BAPTIST HEALTH MEDICAL GROUP PATIENT CONDITIONS AND CONSENTS

1. CONSENT TO DIAGNOSTIC TESTS, PROCEDURES, & MEDICAL TREATMENT:

| voluntarily consent to care involving routine diagnostic tests, procedures and medical treatment as ordered by my treating physician(s), including their assistants or designees. In the case of diagnostic studies, | consent to the interpretation of the studies at an off-site location through the use of telehealth. | consent to medical, nursing, allied health and other students observing and participating in my care under the supervision of a qualified professional. | consent to photographic recordings or reproducible images during the surgical, medical, and/or diagnostic procedure(s) and their use for scientific, educational, identification, or research purposes. | also consent to testing for communicable and blood-borne infectious diseases, such as hepatitis, tuberculosis, and the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV), if a provider orders testing for diagnostic purposes or if there has been an exposure to healthcare personnel. | have been given no guarantees about the results that may be obtained from my care.

2. PHYSICIANS AND OTHER HEALTH CARE PROVIDERS WHO ARE NOT EMPLOYEES OF BAPTIST HEALTH MEDICAL GROUP:

In most situations, the medical treatment provided to patients by Baptist Health Medical Group (“Baptist”) is provided by Baptist employed physicians and providers. However, there other providers who are not employees or agents of Baptist that may provide medical services or be involved in my care. Such providers include, but are not limited to radiologists, pathologists, and psychologists. These providers may be independent or may be employed by other health care organizations. You may receive a separate bill for the services of these providers or the bill you receive may have separate charges for services of such providers. Charges for such services are established by these providers.

3. CONSENT TO USE AND DISCLOSURE OF INFORMATION:

| authorize Baptist to use and disclose information about me to carry out treatment, payment and healthcare operations. Examples of such uses and disclosures include, but are not limited to, providing information to: (a) any person, company, or entity (such as HMOs, insurance companies, workers’ compensation carriers, my employer, the Medicare and Medicaid programs and their intermediaries and review organizations, and any other payor or its review organization or third party administrator) that is or may be liable for paying any claim for benefits arising out of services provided to me by Baptist; (b) any health care provider or ambulance company providing services for me; (c) any providers who may be providing ongoing care to me such as hospitals, home health agencies, nursing homes, physicians, and other health care facilities; (d) any licensing or accrediting organizations necessary for Baptist to obtain or maintain licensure or accreditation; (e) to providers or entities for the purposes of conducting medical audits, utilization reviews, or quality assurance reviews; and (f) any other persons or entities described in Baptist’s Notice of Privacy Practices. This consent includes the release of medical records and billing information related to drug-related conditions, alcoholism, psychological and psychiatric conditions, and/or communicable or blood-borne infectious diseases, such as hepatitis, tuberculosis, and the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV). | also consent to any third party payor or its review organization paying for my care to discuss my plan of treatment for utilization review purposes. | also authorize the release of my social security number to the manufacturer of any implanted medical device in accordance with the Medical Device Tracking Act.

4. FINANCIAL AGREEMENT AND ASSIGNMENT OF BENEFITS:

| agree that | am responsible for payment of my physician office bill. Payment of any portion of my bill not covered by a third party payor is due at the time of service unless Baptist has agreed to other arrangements.

| agree to the assignment of all third party payor benefits to Baptist and to any health care provider rendering services to me. | agree to pay Baptist and other health care providers for all charges for services that are not covered or paid by any third party payor regardless of the reason, including but not limited to a determination by any third party payor that such services are not covered services or medically necessary. To the extent | ama Medicare or Medicaid beneficiary, | certify that any information | provide in applying for payment under Title XVIII (Medicare) or Title XIX (Medicaid) of the Social Security Act is correct. | request payment of authorized benefits to be made on my behalf to Baptist or to any health care provider rendering services to me by the Medicare or Medicaid program. | hereby irrevocably appoint Baptist as my authorized representative to pursue any claims, penalties, and administrative and/or legal remedies on my behalf for collection against any responsible payer, employer-sponsored medical benefit plans, third party liability carrier or, any other responsible third party for any and all benefits due me for the payment of charges associated with my treatment. This assignment shall not be construed as an obligation of Baptist to pursue any such right of recovery. | agree to take all actions necessary to assist Baptist in collecting payment from any such third party payer.

After reasonable notice, any unpaid account may be turned over to a collection agency and/or attorney for collection. Should it be necessary for Baptist to pursue collection, | agree to pay all reasonable collection costs, including court costs and attorney’s fees incurred by Baptist in collecting my account.

BAPTIST HEALTH MEDICAL GROUP PATIENT CONDITIONS AND CONSENTS

1. CONSENT TO DIAGNOSTIC TESTS, PROCEDURES, & MEDICAL TREATMENT:

| voluntarily consent to care involving routine diagnostic tests, procedures and medical treatment as ordered by my treating physician(s), including their assistants or designees. | consent to receive medical care through the use of telehealth and/or remote patient monitoring. | further consent to the interpretation of diagnostic studies from an off-site location using telehealth technologies. | consent to medical, nursing, allied health and other students observing and participating in my care under the supervision of a qualified professional. | consent to photographic recordings or reproducible images during the surgical, medical, and/or diagnostic procedure(s) and their use for scientific, educational, identification, or research purposes. | also consent to testing for communicable and blood-borne infectious diseases, such as hepatitis, tuberculosis, and the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV), if a provider orders testing for diagnostic purposes or if there has been an exposure to healthcare personnel. | have been given no guarantees about the results that may be obtained from my care.

2. PHYSICIANS AND OTHER HEALTH CARE PROVIDERS WHO ARE NOT EMPLOYEES OF BAPTIST HEALTH MEDICAL GROUP:

In most situations, the medical treatment provided to patients by Baptist Health Medical Group (“Baptist”) is provided by Baptist employed physicians and providers. However, there other providers who are not employees or agents of Baptist that may provide medical services or be involved in my care. Such providers include, but are not limited to radiologists, pathologists, and psychologists. These providers may be independent or may be employed by other health care organizations. You may receive a separate bill for the services of these providers or the bill you receive may have separate charges for services of such providers. Charges for such services are established by these providers.

3. FINANCIAL AGREEMENT AND ASSIGNMENT OF BENEFITS:

| agree that | am responsible for payment of my physician office bill. Payment of any portion of my bill not covered by a third party payor is due at the time of service unless Baptist has agreed to other arrangements.

| agree to the assignment of all third party payor benefits to Baptist and to any health care provider rendering services to me. | agree to pay Baptist and other health care providers for all charges for services that are not covered or paid by any third party payor regardless of the reason, including but not limited to a determination by any third party payor that such services are not covered services or medically necessary. | acknowledge and agree that Baptist is not required to accept assignment of any third party payer benefits, in which case, | may receive a bill from Baptist for the full amount of charges related to any care or treatment provided to me or my guarantor by Baptist and | agree to pay Baptist for such charges. Moreover, | understand that Baptist may accept payment from payers with whom it does not have a contract and that any acceptance of payment does not constitute acceptance by Baptist of any reimbursement rates established by such third party payers and that | may receive a bill from Baptist for the difference between the rate paid by such payers and Baptist’s charges. To the extent | am a Medicare or Medicaid beneficiary, | certify that any information | provide in applying for payment under Title XVIII (Medicare) or Title XIX (Medicaid) of the Social Security Act is correct. | request payment of authorized benefits to be made on my behalf to Baptist or to any health care provider rendering services to me by the Medicare or Medicaid program. | hereby irrevocably appoint Baptist as my authorized representative to pursue any claims, penalties, and administrative and/or legal remedies on my behalf for collection against any responsible payer, employer-sponsored medical benefit plans, third party liability carrier or, any other responsible third party for any and all benefits due me for the payment of charges associated with my treatment. This assignment shall not be construed as an obligation of Baptist to pursue any such right of recovery. | agree to take all actions necessary to assist Baptist in collecting payment from any such third party payer.

After reasonable notice, any unpaid account may be turned over to a collection agency and/or attorney for collection. Should it be necessary for Baptist to pursue collection, | agree to pay all reasonable collection costs, including court costs and attorney’s fees incurred by Baptist in collecting my account.

2. PHYSICIANS AND OTHER HEALTH CARE PROVIDERS WHO ARE NOT EMPLOYEES OF BAPTIST HEALTH MEDICAL GROUP:

In most situations, the medical treatment provided to patients by Baptist Health Medical Group (‘Baptist’) is provided by Baptist employed physicians and providers. However, there are other providers who are not employees or agents of Baptist that may provide medical services or be involved in my care. Such providers include, but are not limited to radiologists, pathologists, and psychologists. These providers may be independent or may be employed by other health care organizations. You may receive a separate bill for the services of these providers or the

bill you receive may have separate charges for services of such providers. Charges for such services are established by these providers.

3. FINANCIAL AGREEMENT AND ASSIGNMENT OF BENEFITS:

| agree that | am responsible for payment of my physician office bill. Payment of any portion of my bill not covered by a third party payor is due at the time of service unless Baptist has agreed to other arrangements.

| agree to the assignment of all third party payor bet efits to Baptist and to any health care provider rendering services to me. | agree to pay Baptist and other health care providers for all charges for services that are not covered or paid by any third party payor regardless of the reason, including but not limited to a determination by any third party payor that such services are not covered services or medically necessary. | acknowledge and agree that Baptist is not required to accept assignment of any third party payer benefits, in which case, I may receive a bill from Baptist for the full amount of charges related to any care or treatment provided to me or my guarantor by Baptist and | agree to pay Baptist for such charges. Moreover, | understand that Baptist may accept payment from payers with whom it does not have a contract and that any acceptance of payment does not constitute acceptance by Baptist of any reimbursement rates established by such third party payers and that | may receive a bill from Baptist for the difference between the rate paid by such payers and Baptist's charges. This assignment shall not be construed as an obligation of Baptist to pursue any such right of recovery. I agree to take all actions necessary to assist Baptist in collecting payment from any such third parfy payer.

After reasonable notice, any unpaid account may be turned over to a collection agency and/or attorney for collection. Should it be necessary for Baptist to pursue collection, | agree to pay all reasonable collection costs, including court costs and attorney's fees incurred by Baptist in collecting my account.

Pursuant to the Fair Credit Reporting Act (15 USG §1681b(a)(2)), | authorize any credit reporting agency engaged by Baptist to release to Baptist or any of its representatives or affiliates, my consumer report. | understand that the purpose of this authorization and requests to obtain my consumer report, which may be used to determine the availability of or the need er eral assistance, charity care, or insurance coverage for me and may be used for billing and collection purposes related to payment for services provided to me. | understand this authorization is valid until my Baptist account for all services is closed.

Baptist maintains a smoke-free environment at each of its locations. Smoking is prohibited by health care personnel, patients, and visitors.

1. CONSENT TO DIAGNOSTIC TESTS, PROCEDURES, & MEDICAL TREATMENT:

| voluntarily consent to care involving routine diagnostic tests, procedures and medical treatment as ordered by my treating physician(s), including their assistants or designees. | consent to receive medical care through the use of telehealth and/or remote patient monitoring. | further consent to the interpretation of diagnostic studies from an off-site location using telehealth technologies. | consent to medical, nursing, allied health and other students observing and participating in my care under the supervision of a qualified professional. | consent to photographic recordings or reproducible images during the surgical, medical, and/or diagnostic procedure(s) and their use for scientific, educational, identification, or research purposes. | also consent to testing for communicable and blood-borne infectious diseases, such as hepatitis, tuberculosis, and the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV), if a provider orders testing for diagnostic purposes or if there has been an exposure to healthcare personnel. | have been given no guarantees about the results that may be obtained from my care.

2. PHYSICIANS AND OTHER HEALTH CARE PROVIDERS WHO ARE NOT EMPLOYEES OF BAPTIST HEALTH MEDICAL GROUP:

In most situations, the medical treatment provided to patients by Baptist Health Medical Group (“Baptist”) is provided by Baptist employed physicians and providers. However, there are other providers who are not employees or agents of Baptist that may provide medical services or be involved in my care. Such providers include, but are not limited to radiologists, pathologists, and psychologists. These providers may be independent or may be employed by other health care organizations. You may receive a separate bill for the services of these providers or the bill you receive may have separate charges for services of such providers. Charges for such services are established by these providers.

3. FINANCIAL AGREEMENT AND ASSIGNMENT OF BENEFITS:

| agree that | am responsible for payment of my physician office bill. Payment of any portion of my bill not covered by a third party payor is due at the time of service unless Baptist has agreed to other arrangements.

| agree to the assignment of all third party payor benefits to Baptist and to any health care provider rendering services to me. | agree to pay Baptist and other health care providers for all charges for services that are not covered or paid by any third party payor regardless of the reason, including but not limited to a determination by any third party payor that such services are not covered services or medically necessary. | acknowledge and agree that Baptist is not required to accept assignment of any third party payer benefits, in which case, | may receive a bill from Baptist for the full amount of charges related to any care or treatment provided to me or my guarantor by Baptist and | agree to pay Baptist for such charges. Moreover, | understand that Baptist may accept payment from payers with whom it does not have a contract and that any acceptance of payment does not constitute acceptance by Baptist of any reimbursement rates established by such third party payers and that | may receive a bill from Baptist for the difference between the rate paid by such payers and Baptist’s charges. aK: 5. The claimant has the residual functional capacity to perform sedentary work as ad defined in 20 CFR 404.1567(a) and 416.967(a) except that he is unable to walk more than a few minutes at a time. He could never climb ladders, ropes, and scaffolds. He could occasionally climb ramps and stairs, balance, stoop, kneel, crouch, and crawl. He must avoid even moderate exposure to temperature extremes, wetness, high humidity, pulmonary irritants, and hazards. He could understand, remember, and carry out simple, routine tasks. He could tolerate occasional, superficial interactions with coworkers and supervisors in an object-focused work setting. He could adapt to occasional changes i ina -

4 routine work environment.

In making this finding, the undersigned has considered all symptoms and the extent to which these symptoms can reasonably be accepted as consistent with the objective medical evidence and other evidence, based on the requirements of 20 CFR 404.1529 and 416.929 and SSR 16-3p. The undersigned also considered the medical opinion(s) and prior administrative medical finding(s) in accordance with the requirements of 20 CFR 404.1520c and 416.920c.

The claimant has the following degree of limitation in the four broad areas of mental furictioning set out in the disability regulations for evaluating mental disorders and in the mental disorders listings in 20 CFR, Part 404, Subpart P, Appendix 1: a mild limitation in understanding, remembering, or applying information; a moderate limitation in interacting with others} 'a mild limitation in concentrating, persisting, or maintaining pace; and a moderate limitation i if adapting or managing oneself. ! joe The claimant has been diagnosed y ‘Wwithrdegenérative: disé: disease, ‘chronic obstructive pulmonary disease; chronic’ ‘bronchitis, obstructive sleep-apnea,-history-Of syncope: and obésity?: He. a complains of low back pain. Diagnostic testing reveals thoracolumbar scoliosis, moderate multilevel degeneration in the thoracic and lumbar spine with spur and osteophyte formation. On examination, he has tenderness, decreased range of motion, decreased reflexes, positive straight leg raising, and antalgic gait. He has been treated with medication, physical therapy, and injections with only transient relief. He also reports shortness of breath, cough, daytime sleepiness, snoring, apneic events, and fatigue. His episodes of coughing have led to syncopal episodes. Pulmonary function test shows moderately severe restriction. CT scan of the'chest reveals scattered air trapping. On examination, he has rhonchi. He has been treated with inhalers and CPAP machine, At 5’9” and 254 lbs., with a body mass index of 37.64, the claimant is obese (Exhibits 12F, 14F, 17F, 20F, 21 F, 22F, 24F, 25F, 27F, 29F & 30F). |

‘The claimant has also been diagnosed with depression and anxiety.. He reports symptoms such as stress, irritability, nervousness, trouble sleeping, and difficulty with concentration. On mental status examination, he has an anxious, depressed, and irtitable mood with anxious affect, He also has pressured speech, fair remote memory, and mildly impaired complex attention,| He has been treated with medication with little improvement (Exhibits 13F, 15F, 28F & 32F). ||

As for the opinion evidence, the consultative examiner opined that the claimant could walk for short distances, but would have no limitation to carrying heavy objects, could stand and) sit without limitation, and could avoid ordinary hazards in the workplace and travel without any limitations (Exhibit 12F). The undersigned finds this opinion persuasive. It is supported by the

Marquess, Douglas James, MD Progress Notes @ Physician Signed

Specialty: Internal Medicine

Encounter Date: [DOB-2988]

Subjective

Chief complaint is annual exam Timothy Ruhl is a 56 y.o. male.

History of Present Illness Tim is here today for his annual exam. He recently got up for respiratory illness. He was treated with some steroids and apparently some antibiotics and is feeling better. He does have non-insulin-dependent diabetes. It has been approximately a year since we have checked his A1c. He is on some Lipitor for his cholesterol. Since his last visit he has lost approximately 20 pounds. We did go over his health maintenance issues. He does not want to receive the shingles vaccine or COVID vaccines. We did counsel

regarding pneumococcal vaccine and flu vaccine. He is going to receive the flu vaccine here today.

The following portions of the patient's history were reviewed and updated as appropriate: allergies, current medications, past family history, past medical history, past social history, past surgical history and problem list.

Review of Systems Constitutional: Negative for chills, fatigue and fever. HENT: Negative for congestion, ear pain, hearing loss, nosebleeds, sore throat, trouble swallowing and voice change. He is complaining of some scratchiness in his throat Eyes: Negative for blurred vision, double vision and visual disturbance. Respiratory: Positive for cough. Negative for chest tightness. He still reports a cough. He does sometimes cough to the point of passing out. Cardiovascular: Negative for chest pain, palpitations and leg swelling. Gastrointestinal: Negative for abdominal pain, blood in stool, constipation, diarrhea, GERD and indigestion. Genitourinary: Negative for dysuria, hematuria and nocturia. Musculoskeletal: Positive for back pain. Neurological: Negative for dizziness, weakness, light-headedness, numbness and headache. He occasionally reports that things would drop out of his right hand.

Author: Wilson, Matthew J, MD Service: Medicine Filed: 06/28/25 2138 Date of Service: 06/28/25 1848 Status: Signed Editor: Wilson, Matthew J, MD (Physician)

Author Type: Physician Creation Time: 06/28/25 1848

EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT ENCOUNTER Room Number: 32/32 PCP: Arla, Mohana Rao, MD Independent Historians: Patient and Family

HPI: Chief Complaint: had concerns including Neck Pain.

Context: Timothy Ruhl is a 58 y.o. male with a medical history of bipolar, HTN, COPD, diabetes who presents to the ED c/o acute right-sided neck pain. Patient states symptoms began 3 days ago and were mild at first but have gradually worsened in intensity. Patient states tonight the pain is so intense he cannot sleep or do anything. Upon arrival patient began screaming and grabbing his head further questioning states the pain is all located to his right trapezius distribution. No known injury.

Review of prior external notes (non-ED) -and- Review of prior external test results outside of this encounter: Office visit with internal medicine from earlier today reviewed and notable for presentation secondary to neck pain. Patient was noted to have tenderness in the right trapezius which was painful even to light touch patient sent to the ER.

Prescription drug monitoring program review: KASPER reviewed by Wilson, Matthew J, MD

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY Active Ambulatory Problems

CT Head Without Contrast

CT Head Without Contrast, CT Cervical Spine Without Contrast

Result Date: 6/28/2025

NON-CONTRAST CT HEAD & CT CERVICAL SPINE REASON: Headache and neck pain, no known injury COMPARISON STUDIES: Previous head and cervical spine CT, 9/14/2023. TECHNIQUE: Axial images were acquired from the skull base to vertex without contrast, including multiplanar reformats, per standard departmental protocol. Routine cervical spine CT without contrast. Multiplanar reformats were created. Radiation dose reduction techniques were utilized, including automated exposure control, and exposure modulation based on body size. FINDINGS: Head CT: There is no CT evidence of acute intracranial hemorrhage, mass, or infarct. There is volume loss, but there is no evidence of hydrocephalus or extra-axial fluid collection. Brain parenchymal density is within normal limits. Skull base and calvarium show no acute abnormality, and the extracranial soft tissues show no acute abnormality. C-spine CT: There is no evidence of acute alignment abnormality. There are spinal degenerative changes, but there is no fracture or other acute bony abnormality. The paraspinous soft tissues show no acute abnormality.

ED Course

Sat Jun 28, 2025

2013 CT head interpreted by me demonstrates no evidence of intracranial hemorrhage

2137 Workup in the emergency department is overall unremarkable. Radiological evaluation of the head and C-spine demonstrate no evidence of acute abnormality. Patient treated with Lidoderm patch, Norco and muscle relaxer. On reassessment patient demonstrates improvement in his symptoms. Explained findings and answered all the patient's questions. Will discharge with a prescription for muscle laxer, Lidoderm patches and a couple Valium. Patient instructed on safe use. Return precautions

Ordering provider: Wilson, Matthew J, MD 06/28/25 1925 Order status: Completed

Resulted by: Vaughan, Thomas E, MD Filed by: Interface, Rad Results Enterprise In 06/28/25 2034 Performed: 06/28/25 2001 - 06/28/25 2011 Accession number: [PHONE-304]

Resulting lab: BAPTIST HEALTH RADIOLOGY

Narrative:

NON-CONTRAST CT HEAD & CT CERVICAL SPINE

REASON: Headache and neck pain, no known injury COMPARISON STUDIES: Previous head and cervical spine CT, 9/14/2023.

TECHNIQUE: Axial images were acquired from the skull base to vertex without contrast, including multiplanar reformats, per standard departmental protocol. Routine cervical spine CT without contrast. Multiplanar reformats were created. Radiation dose reduction techniques were utilized, including automated exposure control, and exposure modulation based on body size.

FINDINGS:

Head CT: There is no CT evidence of acute intracranial hemorrhage, mass, or infarct. There is volume loss, but there is no evidence of

hydrocephalus or extra-axial fluid collection. Brain parenchymal

density is within normal limits. Skull base and calvarium show no acute abnormality, and the extracranial soft tissues show no acute

abnormality.

C-spine CT: There is no evidence of acute alignment abnormality. There are spinal degenerative changes, but there is no fracture or other acute bony abnormality. The paraspinous soft tissues show no acute abnormality.

Impression:

Admin instructions: Call 911. Don't prime. Spray in 1 nostril for overdose. Repeat in 2-3 minutes in other nostril if no or minimal breathing/responsiveness. Modified from: naloxone (NARCAN) 4 MG/0.1ML nasal spray [820784058]

Our mission is to provide you with high quality individualized care that is safe and effective to meet your healthcare needs. We will provide you with education and instruction to help with your pain management/recovery, and we will strive for this to be a successful process. This needs to be a team effort to include not only your care providers at the hospital, but you, the patient.

Welcome to Baptist Health

Our care goals are: e To work together in providing your treatment in a respectful manner. e To help you meet the goals of your treatment plan. e Tomaintain a safe environment for the delivery of health care services at our hospital. e To protect the safety and well-being of all patients, visitors, staff and care providers.

We value you as a patient and want to continue to provide you with high quality safe care and service. To do so, we need to outline the expectations that will foster an effective provider- patient relationship.

The following expectations are required to maintain an effective provider- patient relationship.

Patient Behavior Expectations

e | will comply with the medical treatment plan and recommendations developed between my provider and me.

e | will treat my care providers with dignity and respect, and | expect to be treated with dignity and respect.

e | understand that profanity, physical or verbal abuse, or any other form of violence against anyone will not be allowed or tolerated.

e | understand that unauthorized and/or illegal drugs are not allowed in the hospital.

e For my safety, | will not leave the nursing unit where | am a patient during my treatment unless accompanied by hospital personnel for diagnostic procedures or treatments.

e If | leave the unit, it will be considered going against medical advice and | may be discharged from the hospital. For any future medical care, | would need to consult my primary care MD/Provider or go to an Emergency Room for further evaluation. Hospital staff have the right not to re-admit me for further rai unless an emergency situation exists.

INPATIENT AND OUTPATIENT CONDITIONS AND C

2. PHYSICIANS AND OTHER HEALTH CARE PROVIDERS WHO ARE NOT EMPLOYEES OF THE HOSPITAL: Physicians and certified registered nurse anesthetists rendering medical treatment to patients in the hospital are not employed by the Hospital. In most situations, advanced practitioners, including but not limited to, nurse practitioners, physician assistants, surgical assistants and psychologists, providing treatment to patients in the Hospital are not employed by the Hospital. You may receive a separate bill for the services of these providers, or the Hospital bill you receive may have separate charges for services of such providers. Charges for such services are established by the providers.

3. FINANCIAL AGREEMENT AND ASSIGNMENT OF BENEFITS: | agree that | am responsible for payment of my Hospital bill. Payment of any portion of my bill not covered by a third party payor is due upon discharge from the Hospital unless the Hospital has agreed to other arrangements. | agree to the assignment of all third party payor benefits to the Hospital and to any health care provider rendering services to me. | acknowledge and agree that Hospital is not required to accept assignment of any third party payor benefits, in which case, | may receive a bill from Hospital and | agree to pay my Hospital bill to the full extent permitted under federal and state law. Moreover, | understand that Hospital may accept payment from third party payors with whom it does not have a written signed agreement and that such acceptance of payment does not constitute acceptance by Hospital of any reimbursement rates established by such third party payors and that, to the extent permitted by federal law, | may receive a bill from Hospital for the difference between the rate paid by such third party payors and the Hospital’s charges. | agree to pay the Hospital and other health care providers for all charges for services that are not covered or paid by any third party payor regardless of the reason, including but not limited to a determination by any third party payor that such services are not covered services or medically necessary. Discharge Instructions

Follow-up with your primary care physician within 3 to 4 days for repeat evaluation

Please return to the ER with new or worsening symptoms include but not limited to uncontrolled pain, changes in mental status, inability to ambulate, chest pain, shortness of breath

Please do not drive or operate heavy machinery while using Valium or muscle relaxers

Please do not use Robaxin, Valium or any narcotic pain medication at the same time

Printed on 9/16/25 9:05 AM Page 94




SF oo apristT HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Ruhl, Timothy or 4000 KRESGE WAY MRN: [PHONE-350], DOB: 8/5/1966, Legal Sex: M Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 6/28/2025, D/C: 6/28/2025

[DOB-2735] - ED in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT (continued)

More Information

Muscle Cramps and Spasms

Muscle cramps and spasms occur when a muscle or muscles tighten and you have no control over this tightening (involuntary muscle contraction). They are a common problem that can happen in any muscle. The most common place is in the calf muscles of the leg. There are a few ways that muscle cramps and spasms differ:

e Muscle cramps are painful. They come and go and may last for a few seconds or up to 15 minutes. Muscle cramps are often more forceful and last longer than muscle spasms. e Muscle spasms may or may not be painful. They may last just a few seconds or last much longer.

Certain conditions, such as diabetes or Parkinson's disease, can make you more likely to have cramps or spasms. But in most cases, cramps and spasms are not caused by other conditions. Common causes include:

Overexertion. This is when you do more physical work or exercise than your body is ready for. Overuse from doing the same movements too many times.

Staying in one position for too long.

Improper preparation, form, or technique when playing a sport or doing an activity.

Not enough water or other fluids in your body (dehydration).

Other causes may include:

e Injury.

e Side effects of some medicines.

e Too few salts and minerals in your body (e/ectrolytes), such as potassium and calcium. This could happen if you are taking water pills (diuretics) or if you are pregnant.

In many cases, the cause of muscle cramps or spasms is not known.

Follow these instructions at home: Eating and drinking

e Drink enough fluid to keep your pee (urine) pale yellow. This can help prevent cramps or spasms. e Eat a healthy diet that includes a lot of nutrients to help your muscles work. A healthy diet includes fruits and vegetables, lean protein, whole grains, and low-fat or nonfat dairy products.

Managing pain and stiffness

~~ “| the

Printed on 9/16/25 9:05 AM Page 95




SF oo apristT HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Ruhl, Timothy or 4000 KRESGE WAY MRN: [PHONE-2734], DOB: 8/5/1966, Legal Sex: M Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 6/28/2025, D/C: 6/28/2025

[DOB-2733] - ED in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT (continued)

More Information (continued)

e Try to massage, stretch, and relax the affected muscle. Do this for a few minutes at a time. e lf told, put ice on the muscles. This may help if you are sore or have pain after a cramp or spasm. o Put ice in a plastic bag. o Place a towel between your skin and the bag. o Leave the ice on for 20 minutes, 2—3 times a day. e lf told, apply heat to tight or tense muscles as often as told by your health care provider. Use the heat source that your provider recommends, such as a moist heat pack or a heating pad. o Place a towel between your skin and the heat source. o Leave the heat on for 20-30 minutes. e lf your skin turns bright red, remove the ice or heat right away to prevent skin damage. The risk of damage is higher if you cannot feel pain, heat, or cold. e Take hot showers or baths to help relax tight muscles.

General instructions

e If you are having cramps often, avoid intense exercise for a few days. e Take over-the-counter and prescription medicines only as told by your provider. e Watch for any changes in your symptoms.

Contact a health care provider if:

e Your cramps or spasms get more severe or happen more often. e Your cramps or spasms do not get better over time.

This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss any questions you have with your health care provider.

Document Revised: [DOB-354] Document Reviewed: [DOB-2731] Elsevier Patient Education © 2024 Elsevier Inc.

Call 911 or have someone take you to the Emergency Department if you have any of the following: Sudden numbness or weakness of your face, arm or leg especially on one side of the body Sudden confusion, difficulty speaking or trouble understanding

Changes in your vision or loss of sight in one eye

Sudden severe headache with no known cause

Sudden dizziness, trouble walking, loss of balance or coordination

It is important to seek emergency care right away if you have further stroke symptoms. If you get emergency help quickly, the powerful clot-dissolving medicines can reduce the disabilities caused by a stroke.

2. PHYSICIANS AND OTHER HEALTH CARE PROVIDERS WHO ARE NOT EMPLOYEES OF THE HOSPITAL: Physicians and certified registered nurse anesthetists rendering medical treatment to patients in the hospital are not employed by the Hospital. In most situations, advanced practitioners, including but not limited to, nurse practitioners, physician assistants, surgical assistants and psychologists, providing treatment to patients in the Hospital are not employed by the Hospital. You may receive a separate bill for the services of these providers, or the Hospital bill you receive may have separate charges for services of such providers. Charges for such services are established by the providers.

None

Call 911 or have someone take you to the Emergency Department if you have any of the following: Sudden numbness or weakness of your face, arm or leg especially on one side of the body Sudden confusion, difficulty speaking or trouble understanding

Changes in your vision or loss of sight in one eye

Sudden severe headache with no known cause

Sudden dizziness, trouble walking, loss of balance or coordination

It is important to seek emergency care right away if you have further stroke symptoms. If you get emergency help quickly, the powerful clot-dissolving medicines can reduce the disabilities caused by a stroke.

For more information: American Stroke Association 1-888-4-STROKE www.strokeassociation.org

IF YOU SMOKE, VAPE OR USE TOBACCO PLEASE READ THE FOLLOWING:

Why is smoking bad for me?

Smoking increases the risk of heart disease, lung disease, vascular disease, stroke, and cancer. If you smoke, STOP!

For more information:

Quit Now Kentucky 1-800-QUIT-NOW https://kentucky.quitlogix.org/en-US/

If you feel like life is too tough and are thinking of suicide or injuring yourself, get help right away! e Call or text 988 to speak to someone.

Call 911 or have someone take you to the Emergency Department if you have any of the following: Sudden numbness or weakness of your face, arm or leg especially on one side of the body Sudden confusion, difficulty speaking or trouble understanding

Changes in your vision or loss of sight in one eye

Sudden severe headache with no known cause

Sudden dizziness, trouble walking, loss of balance or coordination

It is important to seek emergency care right away if you have further stroke symptoms. If you get emergency help quickly, the powerful clot-dissolving medicines can reduce the disabilities caused by a stroke.

For more information: American Stroke Association 1-888-4-STROKE www.strokeassociation.org

Call 911 or have someone take you to the Emergency Department if you have any of the following: Sudden numbness or weakness of your face, arm or leg especially on one side of the body Sudden confusion, difficulty speaking or trouble understanding

Changes in your vision or loss of sight in one eye

Sudden severe headache with no known cause

Sudden dizziness, trouble walking, loss of balance or coordination

It is important to seek emergency care right away if you have further stroke symptoms. If you get emergency help quickly, the powerful clot-dissolving medicines can reduce the disabilities caused by a stroke.

For more information: American Stroke Association 1-888-4-STROKE www.strokeassociation.org

[DOB-2275] - Ancillary Orders in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE XRAY (continued) Instructions for After Discharge

Patient Instructions None

Call 911 or have someone take you to the Emergency Department if you have any of the following: Sudden numbness or weakness of your face, arm or leg especially on one side of the body Sudden confusion, difficulty speaking or trouble understanding

Changes in your vision or loss of sight in one eye

Sudden severe headache with no known cause

Sudden dizziness, trouble walking, loss of balance or coordination

It is important to seek emergency care right away if you have further stroke symptoms. If you get emergency help quickly, the powerful clot-dissolving medicines can reduce the disabilities caused by a stroke.

For more information: American Stroke Association 1-888-4-STROKE www.strokeassociation.org

IF YOU SMOKE, VAPE OR USE TOBACCO PLEASE READ THE FOLLOWING: Why is smoking bad for me?

For more information:

Quit Now Kentucky 1-800-QUIT-NOW https://kentucky.quitlogix.org/en-US/

If you feel like life is too tough and are thinking of suicide or injuring yourself, get help right away! e Call or text 988 to speak to someone.

[DOB-2196] - Transcribe Orders in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE PATIENT ACCESS (continued) Patient Instructions (continued)

None

Call 911 or have someone take you to the Emergency Department if you have any of the following: Sudden numbness or weakness of your face, arm or leg especially on one side of the body Sudden confusion, difficulty speaking or trouble understanding

Changes in your vision or loss of sight in one eye

Sudden severe headache with no known cause

Sudden dizziness, trouble walking, loss of balance or coordination

It is important to seek emergency care right away if you have further stroke symptoms. If you get emergency help quickly, the powerful clot-dissolving medicines can reduce the disabilities caused by a stroke.

For more information: American Stroke Association 1-888-4-STROKE www.strokeassociation.org

IF YOU SMOKE, VAPE OR USE TOBACCO PLEASE READ THE FOLLOWING:

Why is smoking bad for me?

Smoking increases the risk of heart disease, lung disease, vascular disease, stroke, and cancer. If you smoke, STOP!

For more information:

Quit Now Kentucky 1-800-QUIT-NOW https://kentucky.quitlogix.org/en-US/

If you feel like life is too tough and are thinking of suicide or injuring yourself, get help right away! e Call or text 988 to speak to someone.

ED Provider Notes by Wilson, Matthew J, MD at 9/14/2023 1507

Author: Wilson, Matthew J, MD Service: Emergency Medicine Filed: 09/14/23 1752 Date of Service: 09/14/23 1507 Status: Signed Editor: Wilson, Matthew J, MD (Physician)

Author Type: Physician Creation Time: 09/14/23 1507

EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT ENCOUNTER

Room Number: 15/15 PCP: Marquess, Douglas James, MD Historian: Patient, wife

HPI: Chief Complaint: Back pain, head pain

Context: Timothy Ruhl is a 57 y.o. male who presents to the ED c/o back pain, head pain. Patient was involved in an altercation earlier this afternoon where he was assaulted by 2 individuals per his report. Patient states he was struck on his head and states his head bounced on the pavement twice. Patient is not on any blood thinners. Patient is uncertain if he lost consciousness. Patient states he has chronic lumbar back pain and is scheduled for pain injections next week. Patient states he is having pain throughout his entire. Patient additionally has some tenderness in the right anterior chest wall.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY Active Ambulatory Problems Diagnosis ¢ Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD)

Result Date: 9/14/2023

CT HEAD, CERVICAL SPINE, THORACIC SPINE AND LUMBAR SPINE WITHOUT CONTRAST HISTORY: Status post assault, headache, neck and back pain. Dizziness. COMPARISON: None. CT HEAD WITHOUT CONTRAST: The brain and ventricles are symmetrical. There is no evidence of hemorrhage, hydrocephalus or of abnormal extra- axial fluid. Bone windows showed no evidence of a calvarial fracture. No focal area of decreased attenuation to suggest acute infarction or contusion is appreciated. A small scalp hematoma is noted in the parieto-occipital region posteriorly to the left of midline. CT cervical spine without contrast: The alignment of the cervical spine is within normal limits. Large anterior osteophytes are appreciated from C4-5 to C6-7. There is no evidence of prevertebral edema or of fracture. C2-3: A small central disc bulge is present. Mild facet degenerative disease is present on the left. C3-4: A small central disc bulge is present. Moderate facet degenerative disease is present on the left which contributes to moderate foraminal stenosis on the left. C4-5: Mild central disc bulge is present. There is no evidence of herniation. C5-6: A right paracentral disc osteophyte complex is present with no evidence of herniation. Mild uncovertebral degenerative disease is present on the right. C6-7: Beam-hardening artifact limits evaluation of the spinal canal. There is a suggestion of a mild to moderate central disc osteophyte complex which may result in mild to moderate canal stenosis. Mild to moderate foraminal stenosis is present on the left secondary to uncovertebral degenerative disease. C7-T1: Evaluation of the spinal canal is limited by beam hardening artifact from patient's shoulders.

Beam-hardening artifact limits evaluation of the lower cervical spine. There is no evidence of fracture. Multilevel degenerative disease is noted as described above. There may be mild to moderate canal stenosis at C6-7 secondary to a central disc osteophyte complex. Clinical correlation suggested. Further evaluation could be performed with a MRI examination of the cervical spine as indicated. See above. CT thoracic spine without contrast: Diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis is appreciated with anterior bridging osteophyte noted extending from T3-L1. There is no evidence of fracture. Evaluation of the spinal canal is hampered somewhat by technique but no obvious disc herniation is appreciated. IMPRESSION: There is no evidence of fracture. Anterior bridging osteophyte is noted from T3-L1. CT lumbar spine without contrast: The alignment of the lumbar spine is within normal limits. There is no evidence of an acute fracture. A prominent osteophyte is noted arising from the anterior aspect of the L3 vertebral body superiorly and there is lucency related to its base consistent with a fracture. There is adjacent cortication suggesting that this is not an acute fracture but may represent a subacute to remote fracture. The lucency was present on the CT examination of the abdomen and pelvis performed on [DOB-1023]. L1-L2: There is no evidence of disc bulge or herniation. L2-L3: A mild broad-based disc osteophyte complex is present which is more prominent far laterally to the right where it contributes to mild foraminal stenosis. L3-L4: Mild facet degenerative disease is present bilaterally. L4-L5: There is moderate canal stenosis secondary to a broad-based disc osteophyte complex which, when combined with posterior element degenerative disease results in moderate canal stenosis and moderate lateral recess narrowing bilaterally. The lateral recess narrowing appears to be slightly more prominent to the left. L5-S1: Transitional anatomy is appreciated with partial sacralization of L5. Mild facet degenerative disease is present bilaterally. Mild to moderate degenerative disease involving the sacroiliac joints is appreciated bilaterally. IMPRESSION: 1. There is partial sacralization of L5. Careful correlation prior to any intervention is required given the presence of transitional anatomy.

MEDICATIONS GIVEN IN ER Medications HYDROcodone-acetaminophen (NORCO) 5-325 MG per tablet 1 tablet (1 tablet Oral Given 9/14/23 1731)

MEDICAL DECISION MAKING, PROGRESS, and CONSULTS

57-year-old male presenting for evaluation following an assault. Patient reportedly assaulted by 2 individuals while trying to get them to move his truck. Patient was dropped on his back and had by these individuals. Patient is uncertain if he lost consciousness. Patient palpated from head to toe with only identifiable injuries—tenderness to palpation throughout the thoracic and lumbar spines without specific point tenderness, mild abrasion on the left posterior occiput, mild tenderness to palpation in the left anterior chest wall. Radiological evaluation demonstrates intracranial hemorrhage, no acute fractures or malalignment. There are some chronic appearing abnormalities located in the lumbar spine the patient is aware of. Patient will be seen pain management next week. Patient counseled extensively on supportive care measures and slow return to activity. Patient is agreeable with the plan. Patient discharged to follow-up with primary care physician and pain management.

All labs have been independently reviewed by me. All radiology studies have been reviewed by me and | have also

reviewed the radiology report. EKG's independently viewed and interpreted by me. Discussion below represents my analysis of pertinent findings related to patient's condition, differential diagnosis, treatment plan and final disposition.

Additional sources: - Discussed/ obtained information from independent historians: Wife

- External (non-ED) record review: Office visit with family medicine from 8/31/2023 reviewed and notable for back pain for baclofen, Medrol Dosepak and follow-up.

- Chronic or social conditions impacting care: Chronic back pain Orders placed during this visit: Orders Placed This Encounter

CT Cervical Spine Without Contrast [622824352] Resulted: 09/14/23 1651, Result status: Final result Ordering provider: Wilson, Matthew J, MD 09/14/23 1515 Order status: Completed Resulted by: Anvar, Stephen A, MD Filed by: Interface, Rad Results Enterprise In 09/14/23 1654 Performed: 09/14/23 1533 - 09/14/23 1548 Accession number: [PHONE-1058] Resulting lab: BAPTIST HEALTH RADIOLOGY Narrative: CT HEAD, CERVICAL SPINE, THORACIC SPINE AND LUMBAR SPINE WITHOUT CONTRAST

HISTORY: Status post assault, headache, neck and back pain. Dizziness. COMPARISON: None.

CT HEAD WITHOUT CONTRAST: The brain and ventricles are symmetrical. There is no evidence of hemorrhage, hydrocephalus or of abnormal extra-axial fluid. Bone windows showed no evidence of a calvarial

fracture. No focal area of decreased attenuation to suggest acute

infarction or contusion is appreciated. A small scalp hematoma is noted

in the parieto-occipital region posteriorly to the left of midline.

CT cervical spine without contrast: The alignment of the cervical spine is within normal limits. Large anterior osteophytes are appreciated from C4-5 to C6-7. There is no evidence of prevertebral edema or of fracture.

C2-3: A small central disc bulge is present. Mild facet degenerative disease is present on the left.

C3-4: A small central disc bulge is present. Moderate facet degenerative disease is present on the left which contributes to moderate foraminal stenosis on the left.

C4-5: Mild central disc bulge is present. There is no evidence of herniation.

C5-6: A right paracentral disc osteophyte complex is present with no evidence of herniation. Mild uncovertebral degenerative disease is present on the right.

C6-7: Beam-hardening artifact limits evaluation of the spinal canal. There is a suggestion of a mild to moderate central disc osteophyte complex which may result in mild to moderate canal stenosis. Mild to moderate foraminal stenosis is present on the left secondary to uncovertebral degenerative disease.

C7-T1: Evaluation of the spinal canal is limited by beam hardening artifact from patient's shoulders.

Impression:

Beam-hardening artifact limits evaluation of the lower

cervical spine. There is no evidence of fracture. Multilevel

degenerative disease is noted as described above. There may be mild to moderate canal stenosis at C6-7 secondary to a central disc osteophyte complex. Clinical correlation suggested. Further evaluation could be performed with a MRI examination of the cervical spine as indicated. See above.

CT thoracic spine without contrast: Diffuse idiopathic skeletal

Page 213




[DOB-1059] - ED in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT (continued)

Imaging (continued)

hyperostosis is appreciated with anterior bridging osteophyte noted extending from T3-L1. There is no evidence of fracture. Evaluation of

the spinal canal is hampered somewhat by technique but no obvious disc herniation is appreciated.

IMPRESSION: There is no evidence of fracture. Anterior bridging osteophyte is noted from T3-L1.

CT lumbar spine without contrast: The alignment of the lumbar spine is within normal limits. There is no evidence of an acute fracture. A prominent osteophyte is noted arising from the anterior aspect of the L3 vertebral body superiorly and there is lucency related to its base consistent with a fracture. There is adjacent cortication suggesting

that this is not an acute fracture but may represent a subacute to remote fracture. The lucency was present on the CT examination of the abdomen and pelvis performed on [DOB-1060].

L1-L2: There is no evidence of disc bulge or herniation.

L2-L3: A mild broad-based disc osteophyte complex is present which is more prominent far laterally to the right where it contributes to mild foraminal stenosis.

L3-L4: Mild facet degenerative disease is present bilaterally.

L4-L5: There is moderate canal stenosis secondary to a broad-based disc osteophyte complex which, when combined with posterior element degenerative disease results in moderate canal stenosis and moderate lateral recess narrowing bilaterally. The lateral recess narrowing

appears to be slightly more prominent to the left.

L5-S1: Transitional anatomy is appreciated with partial sacralization of L5. Mild facet degenerative disease is present bilaterally.

Mild to moderate degenerative disease involving the sacroiliac joints is appreciated bilaterally.

IMPRESSION:

1. There is partial sacralization of L5. Careful correlation prior to

any intervention is required given the presence of transitional anatomy. 2. There is no evidence of an acute fracture. Note is made of lucency consistent with a subacute to remote fracture is noted at the base of a prominent osteophyte arising from the anterior and superior aspect of L3. This was present on the CT examination of the abdomen and pelvis performed on [DOB-1061].

3. Multilevel degenerative disease is noted as described above including moderate canal stenosis and lateral recess narrowing bilaterally at L4-L5. See above. Further evaluation could be performed with a MRI examination of the cervical/thoracic/lumbar spine as indicated.

Radiation dose reduction techniques were utilized, including automated exposure control and exposure modulation based on body size.

This report was finalized on 9/14/2023 4:51 PM by Dr. Stephen Anvar, M.D.

Imaging (continued)

There is no evidence of hemorrhage, hydrocephalus or of abnormal extra-axial fluid. Bone windows showed no evidence of a calvarial fracture. No focal area of decreased attenuation to suggest acute infarction or contusion is appreciated. A small scalp hematoma is noted in the parieto-occipital region posteriorly to the left of midline.

CT cervical spine without contrast: The alignment of the cervical spine is within normal limits. Large anterior osteophytes are appreciated from C4-5 to C6-7. There is no evidence of prevertebral edema or of fracture.

C2-3: A small central disc bulge is present. Mild facet degenerative disease is present on the left.

C3-4: A small central disc bulge is present. Moderate facet degenerative disease is present on the left which contributes to moderate foraminal stenosis on the left.

C4-5: Mild central disc bulge is present. There is no evidence of herniation.

C5-6: A right paracentral disc osteophyte complex is present with no evidence of herniation. Mild uncovertebral degenerative disease is present on the right.

C6-7: Beam-hardening artifact limits evaluation of the spinal canal. There is a suggestion of a mild to moderate central disc osteophyte complex which may result in mild to moderate canal stenosis. Mild to moderate foraminal stenosis is present on the left secondary to uncovertebral degenerative disease.

C7-T1: Evaluation of the spinal canal is limited by beam hardening artifact from patient's shoulders.

Impression:

Beam-hardening artifact limits evaluation of the lower

cervical spine. There is no evidence of fracture. Multilevel

degenerative disease is noted as described above. There may be mild to moderate canal stenosis at C6-7 secondary to a central disc osteophyte complex. Clinical correlation suggested. Further evaluation could be performed with a MRI examination of the cervical spine as indicated. See above.

CT thoracic spine without contrast: Diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis is appreciated with anterior bridging osteophyte noted extending from T3-L1. There is no evidence of fracture. Evaluation of

the spinal canal is hampered somewhat by technique but no obvious disc herniation is appreciated.

IMPRESSION: There is no evidence of fracture. Anterior bridging osteophyte is noted from T3-L1.

CT lumbar spine without contrast: The alignment of the lumbar spine is within normal limits. There is no evidence of an acute fracture. A prominent osteophyte is noted arising from the anterior aspect of the L3 vertebral body superiorly and there is lucency related to its base consistent with a fracture. There is adjacent cortication suggesting

that this is not an acute fracture but may represent a subacute to remote fracture. The lucency was present on the CT examination of the abdomen and pelvis performed on [DOB-1994].

L1-L2: There is no evidence of disc bulge or herniation.

L2-L3: A mild broad-based disc osteophyte complex is present which is

Imaging (continued)

more prominent far laterally to the right where it contributes to mild foraminal stenosis.

L3-L4: Mild facet degenerative disease is present bilaterally.

L4-L5: There is moderate canal stenosis secondary to a broad-based disc osteophyte complex which, when combined with posterior element degenerative disease results in moderate canal stenosis and moderate lateral recess narrowing bilaterally. The lateral recess narrowing

appears to be slightly more prominent to the left.

L5-S1: Transitional anatomy is appreciated with partial sacralization of L5. Mild facet degenerative disease is present bilaterally.

Mild to moderate degenerative disease involving the sacroiliac joints is appreciated bilaterally.

IMPRESSION:

1. There is partial sacralization of L5. Careful correlation prior to

any intervention is required given the presence of transitional anatomy. 2. There is no evidence of an acute fracture. Note is made of lucency consistent with a subacute to remote fracture is noted at the base of a prominent osteophyte arising from the anterior and superior aspect of L3. This was present on the CT examination of the abdomen and pelvis performed on [DOB-1990].

3. Multilevel degenerative disease is noted as described above including moderate canal stenosis and lateral recess narrowing bilaterally at L4-L5. See above. Further evaluation could be performed with a MRI examination of the cervical/thoracic/lumbar spine as indicated.

Radiation dose reduction techniques were utilized, including automated exposure control and exposure modulation based on body size.

Our mission is to provide you with high quality individualized care that is safe and effective to meet your healthcare needs. We will provide you with education and instruction to help with your pain management/recovery, and we will strive for this to be a successful process. This needs to be a team effort to include not only your care providers at the hospital, but you, the patient.

Our care goals are: e To work together in providing your treatment in a respectful manner.

e To help you meet the goals of your treatment plan. e To maintain a safe environment for the delivery of health care services at our hospital. e To protect the safety and well-being of all patients, visitors, staff and care providers.

We value you as a patient and want to continue to provide you with high quality safe care and service. To do so, we need to outline the expectations that will foster an effective provider- patient relationship.

The following expectations are required to maintain an effective provider- patient relationship.

Patient Behavior Expectations e | will comply with the medical treatment plan and recommendations developed between my provider

and me.

e | will treat my care providers with dignity and respect, and | expect to be treated with dignity and respect.

e | understand that profanity, physical or verbal abuse, or any other form of violence against anyone will not be allowed or tolerated.

e | understand that unauthorized and/or illegal drugs are not allowed in the hospital.

e For my safety, | will not leave the nursing unit where | am a patient during my treatment unless accompanied by hospital personnel for diagnostic procedures or treatments.

e If| leave the unit, it will be considered going against medical advice and | may be discharged from the hospital. For any future medical care, | would need to consult my primary care MD/Provider or go to an Emergency Room for further evaluation. Hospital staff have the right not to re-admit me for further care unless an emergency situation exists.

Baptist Health Request for Designation as a Privacy Patient

You have requested to be designated as a privacy patient for this stay at the facility. This designation means that:

e Your name, location in the facility, and your general condition will not be included in the hospital’s directory or available to individuals contacting the hospital patient information line.

e Your presence in the facility will not be shared with clergy or other persons from outside the facility who may ask for you by name including your relatives and friends.

e Flowers and mail directed to the hospital in your name may be returned to the sender.

e If you or your significant others tell or relay your personal information to others, including your location in the facility for visitors, the hospital cannot ensure a higher level of privacy for you.

SF oo apristT HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Ruhl, Timothy or 4000 KRESGE WAY MRN: [PHONE-1932], DOB: 8/5/1966, Legal Sex: M Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 9/14/2023, D/C: 9/14/2023

[DOB-1931] - ED in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT (continued)

More Information

There are many types of head injuries. They can be as minor as a small bump. Some head injuries can be worse. Worse injuries include:

e A strong hit to the head that shakes the brain back and forth, causing damage (concussion).

e A bruise (contusion) of the brain. This means there is bleeding in the brain that can cause swelling.

e Acracked skull (skull fracture).

e Bleeding in the brain that gathers, gets thick (makes a clot), and forms a bump (hematoma).

Most problems from a head injury come in the first 24 hours. However, you may still have side effects up to 7-10 days after your injury. It is important to watch your condition for any changes. You may need to be watched in the emergency department or urgent care, or you may need to stay in the hospital.

What are the causes? There are many possible causes of a head injury. A serious head injury may be caused by:

A car accident.

Bicycle or motorcycle accidents. Sports injuries.

Falls.

Being hit by an object.

What are the signs or symptoms?

Symptoms of a head injury include a bruise, bump, or bleeding where the injury happened. Other physical symptoms may include:

Headache. Feeling like you may vomit (nauseous) or vomiting. Dizziness. Blurred or double vision. Being uncomfortable around bright lights or loud noises. Feeling tired. Trouble waking up. Severe symptoms such as: o Feeling weak or numb on one side of the body.

Printed on 9/16/25 9:05 AM Page 249




SF oo apristT HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Ruhl, Timothy or 4000 KRESGE WAY MRN: [PHONE-1156], DOB: 8/5/1966, Legal Sex: M Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 9/14/2023, D/C: 9/14/2023

[DOB-1929] - ED in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT (continued)

More Information (continued)

Slurred speech.

Swallowing problems.

Fainting.

Shaking movements that you cannot control (seizures).

Oo O00 0

Mental or emotional symptoms may include:

Feeling grumpy or cranky.

Confusion and memory problems.

Having trouble paying attention or concentrating. Changes in eating or sleeping habits.

Feeling worried or nervous (anxious).

Feeling sad (depressed).

How is this treated?

Treatment for this condition depends on how bad the injury is and the type of injury you have. The main goal is to prevent problems and to allow the brain time to heal.

Mild head injury If you have a mild head injury, you may be sent home, and treatment may include:

Being watched. A responsible adult should stay with you for 24 hours after your injury and check on you often. Physical rest.

Brain rest.

Pain medicines.

Very bad head injury

If you have a very bad head injury, treatment may include:

e Being watched closely. This includes staying in the hospital. e Medicines to: o Help with pain. o Prevent seizures. o Help with brain swelling. e Protecting your airway and using a machine that helps you breathe (ventilator). e Watching for and manage swelling inside the brain. e Brain surgery. This may be needed to: o Remove a collection of blood or blood clots. o Stop the bleeding. o Remove a part of the skull. This allows room for the brain to swell.

Follow these instructions at home: Activity

Printed on 9/16/25 9:05 AM Page 250




SF oo apristT HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Ruhl, Timothy or 4000 KRESGE WAY MRN: [PHONE-1158], DOB: 8/5/1966, Legal Sex: M Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 9/14/2023, D/C: 9/14/2023

[DOB-1927] - ED in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT (continued)

More Information (continued)

Rest. Avoid activities that are hard or tiring. Make sure you get enough sleep. Let your brain rest. Do fewer activities that need a lot of thought or attention, such as: o Watching TV. o Playing memory games and doing puzzles. o Job-related work or homework. o Working on the computer, social media, and texting. e Avoid activities that could cause another head injury until your doctor says it is okay. This includes playing sports. e Ask your doctor when it is safe for you to go back to your normal activities, such as work or school. e Ask your doctor when you can drive, ride a bicycle, or use machines. Do not do these activities if you are dizzy.

Lifestyle

Do not drink alcohol until your doctor says it is okay.

Do not use drugs.

If it is hard to remember things, write them down.

If you are easily distracted, try to do one thing at a time.

Talk with family members or close friends when making important decisions.

Tell your friends, family, a trusted co-worker, and work manager about your injury, symptoms, and limits (restrictions). Have them watch for any problems that are new or getting worse.

General instructions

e Take over-the-counter and prescription medicines only as told by your doctor.

e Have a responsible adult stay with you for 24 hours after your head injury. This person should watch you for any changes in your symptoms and be ready to get help.

e Keep all follow-up visits to catch any new problems early.

How is this prevented?

Having another head injury can be dangerous. Another injury can lead to brain damage, brain swelling, or death. You can avoid this by:

e Working on your balance and strength. This can help you avoid falls. e Wearing a seat belt when you are in a moving vehicle. e Wearing a helmet when you:

o Ride a bicycle.

SF oo apristT HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Ruhl, Timothy or 4000 KRESGE WAY MRN: [PHONE-1160], DOB: 8/5/1966, Legal Sex: M Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 9/14/2023, D/C: 9/14/2023

More Information (continued)

o Ski. o Do any other sport or activity that has a risk of injury. e Making your home safer by: o Getting rid of clutter from the floors and stairs. This includes things that can make you trip. Using grab bars in bathrooms and handrails by stairs. Placing non-slip mats on floors and in bathtubs. Putting more light in dim areas.

Oo 0 O

Where to find more information

e Brain Injury Association: biausa.org

Contact a doctor if:

e These symptoms do not go away: Headaches.

Dizziness.

Double vision or vision changes. Trouble sleeping.

Changes in mood.

e You have new symptoms.

(e)

[e) [e) [e) [e)

Get help right away if:

e You have sudden:

Headache that is very bad.

Vomiting that does not stop.

Changes in the size of one of your pupils. Pupils are the black centers of your eyes. Changes in how you see (vision).

More confusion or more grumpy moods.

e You have a seizure.

e Your symptoms get worse.

e You have a clear or bloody fluid coming from your nose or ears.

oO O0O0 0 0

These symptoms may be an emergency. Get help right away. Call 911. e Do not wait to see if the symptoms will go away. e Do not drive yourself to the hospital.

This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss any questions you have with your health care provider.

SF oo apristT HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Ruhl, Timothy or 4000 KRESGE WAY MRN: [PHONE-1164], DOB: 8/5/1966, Legal Sex: M Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 9/14/2023, D/C: 9/14/2023

[DOB-1165] - ED in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT (continued)

More Information (continued)

Acute Back Pain, Adult

Acute back pain is sudden and usually short-lived. It is often caused by an injury to the muscles and tissues in the back. The injury may result from:

e A muscle, tendon, or ligament getting overstretched or torn. Ligaments are tissues that connect bones to each other. Lifting something improperly can cause a back strain.

e Wear and tear (degeneration) of the spinal disks. Spinal disks are circular tissue that provide cushioning between the bones of the spine (vertebrae).

e Twisting motions, such as while playing sports or doing yard work.

e Ahit to the back.

e Arthritis.

You may have a physical exam, lab tests, and imaging tests to find the cause of your pain. Acute back pain usually goes away with rest and home care.

Follow these instructions at home: Managing pain, stiffness, and swelling

e Take over-the-counter and prescription medicines only as told by your health care provider. Treatment may include medicines for pain and inflammation that are taken by mouth or applied to the skin, or muscle relaxants. e Your health care provider may recommend applying ice during the first 24-48 hours after your pain starts. To do this: Put ice in a plastic bag. Place a towel between your skin and the bag. Leave the ice on for 20 minutes, 2—3 times a day. Remove the ice if your skin turns bright red. This is very important. If you cannot feel pain, heat, or cold, you have a greater risk of damage to the area. e lf directed, apply heat to the affected area as often as told by your health care provider. Use the heat source that your health care provider recommends, such as a moist heat pack or a heating pad. o Place a towel between your skin and the heat source. o Leave the heat on for 20-30 minutes. o Remove the heat if your skin turns bright red. This is especially important if you are unable to feel pain, heat, or cold. You have a greater risk of getting burned.

Oo O00 0

Activity

Printed on 9/16/25 9:05 AM Page 253




SF oo apristT HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Ruhl, Timothy or 4000 KRESGE WAY MRN: [PHONE-1166], DOB: 8/5/1966, Legal Sex: M Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 9/14/2023, D/C: 9/14/2023

[DOB-1919] - ED in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT (continued)

More Information (continued)

@ @

genet sree,

>) © <)

Senet a, oes,

Pree,

e Do not stay in bed. Staying in bed for more than 1—2 days can delay your recovery. e Sit up and stand up straight. Avoid leaning forward when you sit or hunching over when you stand. o If you work at a desk, sit close to it so you do not need to lean over. Keep your chin tucked in. Keep your neck drawn back, and keep your elbows bent at a 90-degree angle (right angle). o Sit high and close to the steering wheel when you drive. Add lower back (/umbar) support to your car seat, if needed. e Take short walks on even surfaces as soon as you are able. Try to increase the length of time you walk each day. e Do not sit, drive, or stand in one place for more than 30 minutes at a time. Sitting or standing for long periods of time can put stress on your back. e Do not drive or use heavy machinery while taking prescription pain medicine. e Use proper lifting techniques. When you bend and lift, use positions that put less stress on your back: o Bend your knees. o Keep the load close to your body. o Avoid twisting. e Exercise regularly as told by your health care provider. Exercising helps your back heal faster and helps prevent back injuries by keeping muscles strong and flexible. e Work with a physical therapist to make a safe exercise program, as recommended by your health care provider. Do any exercises as told by your physical therapist.

Lifestyle

e Maintain a healthy weight. Extra weight puts stress on your back and makes it difficult to have good posture. e Avoid activities or situations that make you feel anxious or stressed. Stress and anxiety increase muscle tension and can make back pain worse. Learn ways to manage anxiety and stress, such as through exercise.

General instructions

e Sleep on a firm mattress in a comfortable position. Try lying on your side with your knees slightly bent. If you lie on your back, put a pillow under your knees.

e Keep your head and neck in a straight line with your spine (neutral position) when using electronic equipment like smartphones or pads. To do this:

Printed on 9/16/25 9:05 AM Page 254




SF oo apristT HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Ruhl, Timothy or 4000 KRESGE WAY MRN: [PHONE-1168], DOB: 8/5/1966, Legal Sex: M Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 9/14/2023, D/C: 9/14/2023

[DOB-1917] - ED in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT (continued)

More Information (continued)

o Raise your smartphone or pad to look at it instead of bending your head or neck to look down. o Put the smartphone or pad at the level of your face while looking at the screen. e Follow your treatment plan as told by your health care provider. This may include: o Cognitive or behavioral therapy. o Acupuncture or massage therapy. o Meditation or yoga.

Contact a health care provider if:

You have pain that is not relieved with rest or medicine.

You have increasing pain going down into your legs or buttocks. Your pain does not improve after 2 weeks.

You have pain at night.

You lose weight without trying.

You have a fever or chills.

You develop nausea or vomiting.

You develop abdominal pain.

Get help right away if:

e You develop new bowel or bladder control problems. e You have unusual weakness or numbness in your arms or legs. e You feel faint.

These symptoms may represent a serious problem that is an emergency. Do not wait to see if the symptoms will go away. Get medical help right away. Call your local emergency services (911 in the U.S.). Do not drive yourself to the hospital.

Summary

e Acute back pain is sudden and usually short-lived.

e Use proper lifting techniques. When you bend and lift, use positions that put less stress on your back.

e Take over-the-counter and prescription medicines only as told by your health care provider, and apply heat or ice as told.

This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss any questions you have with your health care provider.

Document Revised: [DOB-1916] Document Reviewed: [DOB-1915] Elsevier Patient Education © 2024 Elsevier Inc.

Call 911 or have someone take you to the Emergency Department if you have any of the following: Sudden numbness or weakness of your face, arm or leg especially on one side of the body Sudden confusion, difficulty speaking or trouble understanding

Changes in your vision or loss of sight in one eye

Sudden severe headache with no known cause

Sudden dizziness, trouble walking, loss of balance or coordination

It is important to seek emergency care right away if you have further stroke symptoms. If you get emergency help quickly, the powerful clot-dissolving medicines can reduce the disabilities caused by a stroke.

For more information: American Stroke Association 1-888-4-STROKE www.strokeassociation.org

IF YOU SMOKE, VAPE OR USE TOBACCO PLEASE READ THE FOLLOWING:

Why is smoking bad for me?

Smoking increases the risk of heart disease, lung disease, vascular disease, stroke, and cancer. If you smoke, STOP!

For more information:

Quit Now Kentucky 1-800-QUIT-NOW https://kentucky.quitlogix.org/en-US/

If you feel like life is too tough and are thinking of suicide or injuring yourself, get help right away! e Call or text 988 to speak to someone.

Author: Davis, Thomas Edward II, MD Service: Emergency Medicine Author Type: Physician Filed: 08/30/23 0037 Date of Service: 08/27/23 1250 Creation Time: 08/27/23 1250 Status: Signed Editor: Davis, Thomas Edward II, MD (Physician)

EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT ENCOUNTER

Room Number: 30/30 PCP: Marquess, Douglas James, MD

HPI:

Chief Complaint: Right flank pain

A complete HPI/ROS/PMH/PSH/SH/FH are unobtainable due to: None

Context: Timothy Ruhl is a 57 y.o. male who presents to the ED c/o right flank pain. Onset of pain 8/15/2030. He thinks it may have occurred from a coughing fit where he was coughing repetitively. Pain radiates to the right lower abdomen. Pain is also worse with moving his right leg. No fever. No vomiting. He has a history of chronic back pain. He states this feels somewhat different than his chronic back pain, however. He has an appointment with his pain management on 9/8/2023. No change in bowel or bladder.
