Resulting lab: BAPTIST HEALTH RADIOLOGY

Narrative:

CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITHOUT IV CONTRAST HISTORY: Right flank pain

TECHNIQUE: Radiation dose reduction techniques were utilized, including automated exposure control and exposure modulation based on body size. 3 mm images were obtained through the abdomen and pelvis without IV contrast.

COMPARISON: CTA chest 8/5/2020

FINDINGS:

A few scattered sub-6 mm pulm nodules within the visualized lung bases, as before. The small bowel is nondistended. The appendix is unremarkable. Mild hepatic steatosis.

Subcentimeter hepatic lesions are too small to characterize. Gallbladder is borderline distended. The pancreas, spleen, adrenal glands and kidneys have an unremarkable noncontrast CT appearance. There is no hydronephrosis or visualized ureteral calculus. No new abdominal pelvic adenopathy by size criteria. Mildly enlarged porta hepatis node measuring 1.2 cm in short axis dimension is grossly unchanged. No free intraperitoneal air is seen findings of avascular necrosis of the

bilateral femoral heads..

Impression:

1. Gallbladder is at the upper limits of normal to borderline

distended. Findings can be seen in the setting of early cholecystitis in the proper context correlation with patient history is recommended.

2. Modified steatosis.

3. Mild thickening of the hepatic flexure which is otherwise under distended. No significant surrounding fat stranding. Findings are likely related to decompression; however, please ensure this patient is up-to-date on colonoscopic screening per current guidelines to exclude any possibility of neoplasm.

4. Other findings as above.

This report was finalized on 8/27/2023 2:51 PM by Dr. Colin Carroll, M.D.

Narrative

Med 377 dispatched non-emergent, no lights and sirens to residence for pt c/o backpain. Upon arrival, pt apartment could not be located for approximately eight minutes. Radio notified of delay and contacted pt for further instructions. Neighbors eventually assisted EMS crew with location. Upon arrival to fourth floor apartment, pt found seated on sofa in obvious pain. Pt stated he had no traumatic event but has had low back pain x 2 weeks and developed a knot in his R lower back for approximately two days with no known cause. Pt has appointment with pain management MD on Sept 8th but pain was so severe today he needed EMS response. Pt did take two prescribed Hydrocode 7.5 mg at approximately 0800 this morning with no relief. Pt back assessed and small knot located in L lower back area. Pt is visibly dry and pale. Pt has recent Hx of bronchitis; lungs auscultated and clear x 4 lobes. Pt does have increased pain in back with inspiration. Pt states pain is 10/10. V/s taken on scene along w/blood glucose. Pt B/p elevated, all other v/s WNL. Pt able to stand with assist x 1 and pivot to sit in awaiting stair-chair and secured with all safety straps without incident. Pt assisted x 2 with EMT Garguilo at the head of chair and EMT Armstrong-Warren on lower extremity handles. Pt again able to stand once on ground level to pivot and sit on stretcher with assistance x 1. Pt secured to stretcher with all safety straps and loaded for transport without incident. Pt pain level did decrease en route with noted B/p becoming more elevated. Pt does state that he has been diagnosed with hypertension. Pt remained stable en route to Baptist ER. Upon arrival, pt triaged by RN Whitney in hallway and assigned to room 30. Pt transferred from stretcher to ER bed via sheet pull x 4 without incident. Pt rails raised and call light within reach. Care turned over to ER staff. EOR.

Our mission is to provide you with high quality individualized care that is safe and effective to meet your healthcare needs. We will provide you with education and instruction to help with your pain management/recovery, and we will strive for this to be a successful process. This needs to be a team effort to include not only your care providers at the hospital, but you, the patient.

Our care goals are: e To work together in providing your treatment in a respectful manner.

e To help you meet the goals of your treatment plan. e To maintain a safe environment for the delivery of health care services at our hospital. e To protect the safety and well-being of all patients, visitors, staff and care providers.

We value you as a patient and want to continue to provide you with high quality safe care and service. To do so, we need to outline the expectations that will foster an effective provider- patient relationship.

The following expectations are required to maintain an effective provider- patient relationship.

Patient Behavior Expectations e | will comply with the medical treatment plan and recommendations developed between my provider

and me.

e | will treat my care providers with dignity and respect, and | expect to be treated with dignity and respect.

e | understand that profanity, physical or verbal abuse, or any other form of violence against anyone will not be allowed or tolerated.

e | understand that unauthorized and/or illegal drugs are not allowed in the hospital.

e For my safety, | will not leave the nursing unit where | am a patient during my treatment unless accompanied by hospital personnel for diagnostic procedures or treatments.

e If| leave the unit, it will be considered going against medical advice and | may be discharged from the hospital. For any future medical care, | would need to consult my primary care MD/Provider or go to an Emergency Room for further evaluation. Hospital staff have the right not to re-admit me for further care unless an emergency situation exists.

| PH)

CONSENT TO DIAGNOSTIC TESTS, PROCEDURES, & MEDICAL TREATMENT: | voluntarily consent to (and consent on behalf of any child born to me while hospitalized) care involving routine diagnostic tests, procedures and medical treatment as ordered by my admitting and treating physicians, including their assistants or designees. | consent to receive medical care through the use of telehealth and/or remote patient monitoring. | further consent to the interpretation of diagnostic studies from an off-site location using telehealth technologies. | consent to medical, nursing, allied health and other students observing and participating in my care under the supervision of a qualified professional. | consent to photographic recordings or reproducible images during the surgical, medical, and/or diagnostic procedure(s) and their use for identification, scientific, educational, or research purposes (I understand that | may request my caregiver that photographs not be taken, unless necessary for my clinical treatment). | also consent to testing for communicable and blood-borne infectious diseases, such as hepatitis, tuberculosis, and the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV), if a provider orders testing for diagnostic purposes or if there has been an exposure to healthcare personnel. | have been given no guarantees about the results that may be obtained from my care. | understand and agree that the results of diagnostic tests, procedures and medical treatment ordered and provided by providers while | am hospitalized, including a notice of admission to the hospital, a notice of discharge from the hospital, and/or notice of transfer from the hospital to another facility will be provided to my primary care physician, physician group and/or post acute care provider on record, if any.

2. PHYSICIANS AND OTHER HEALTH CARE PROVIDERS WHO ARE NOT EMPLOYEES OF THE HOSPITAL: Physicians and certified registered nurse anesthetists rendering medical treatment to patients in the hospital are not employed by the Hospital. In most situations, advanced practitioners, including but not limited to, nurse practitioners, physician assistants, surgical assistants and psychologists, providing treatment to patients in the Hospital are not employed by the Hospital. You may receive a separate bill for the services of these providers, or the Hospital bill you receive may have separate charges for services of such providers. Charges for such services are established by the providers.

3. FINANCIAL AGREEMENT AND ASSIGNMENT OF BENEFITS: | agree that | am responsible for payment of my Hospital bill. SF oo apristT HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Ruhl, Timothy or 4000 KRESGE WAY MRN: [PHONE-1408], DOB: 8/5/1966, Legal Sex: M Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 8/27/2023, D/C: 8/27/2023

[DOB-1677] - ED in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT (continued)

More Information

Sciatica

Sciatica is pain, numbness, weakness, or tingling along the path of the sciatic nerve. The sciatic nerve starts in the lower back and runs down the back of each leg. The nerve controls the muscles in the lower leg and in the back of the knee. It also provides feeling (sensation) to the back of the thigh, the lower leg, and the sole of the foot. Sciatica is a symptom of another medical condition that pinches or puts pressure on the sciatic nerve.

Sciatica most often only affects one side of the body. Sciatica usually goes away on its own or with treatment. In some cases, sciatica may come back (recur).

What are the causes? This condition is caused by pressure on the sciatic nerve or pinching of the nerve. This may be the result of:

A disk in between the bones of the spine bulging out too far (herniated disk). Age-related changes in the spinal disks.

A pain disorder that affects a muscle in the buttock.

Extra bone growth near the sciatic nerve.

A break (fracture) of the pelvis.

Pregnancy.

Tumor. This is rare.

What increases the risk? The following factors may make you more likely to develop this condition:

Playing sports that place pressure or stress on the spine. Having poor strength and flexibility.

A history of back injury or surgery.

Sitting for long periods of time.

Doing activities that involve repetitive bending or lifting. Obesity.

What are the signs or symptoms?

Printed on 9/16/25 9:05 AM Page 312




SF oo apristT HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Ruhl, Timothy or 4000 KRESGE WAY MRN: [PHONE-1410], DOB: 8/5/1966, Legal Sex: M Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 8/27/2023, D/C: 8/27/2023

[DOB-1675] - ED in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT (continued)

More Information (continued)

Symptoms can vary from mild to very severe. They may include:

e Any of the following problems in the lower back, leg, hip, or buttock: o Mild tingling, numbness, or dull aches. o Burning sensations. o Sharp pains.

e Numbness in the back of the calf or the sole of the foot.

e Leg weakness.

e Severe back pain that makes movement difficult.

Symptoms may get worse when you cough, sneeze, or laugh, or when you sit or stand for long periods of time.

How is this diagnosed?

This condition may be diagnosed based on:

Your symptoms and medical history. A physical exam. Blood tests. Imaging tests, such as: o X-rays. o AnMRI. o ACT scan.

How is this treated? In many cases, this condition improves on its own without treatment. However, treatment may include:

Reducing or modifying physical activity. Exercising, including strengthening and stretching. Icing and applying heat to the affected area. Medicines that help to: o Relieve pain and swelling. o Relax your muscles. e Injections of medicines that help to relieve pain and inflammation (steroids) around the sciatic nerve. e Surgery.

Follow these instructions at home: Medicines

e Take over-the-counter and prescription medicines only as told by your health care provider. e Ask your health care provider if the medicine prescribed to you requires you to avoid driving or using heavy machinery.

Managing pain

Printed on 9/16/25 9:05 AM Page 313




SF oo apristT HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Ruhl, Timothy or 4000 KRESGE WAY MRN: [PHONE-1412], DOB: 8/5/1966, Legal Sex: M Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 8/27/2023, D/C: 8/27/2023

[DOB-1673] - ED in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT (continued)

More Information (continued)

e lf directed, put ice on the affected area. To do this: Put ice in a plastic bag. Place a towel between your skin and the bag. Leave the ice on for 20 minutes, 2—3 times a day. If your skin turns bright red, remove the ice right away to prevent skin damage. The risk of skin damage is higher if you cannot feel pain, heat, or cold. e lf directed, apply heat to the affected area as often as told by your health care provider. Use the heat source that your health care provider recommends, such as a moist heat pack or a heating pad. o Place a towel between your skin and the heat source. o Leave the heat on for 20-30 minutes. o If your skin turns bright red, remove the heat right away to prevent burns. The risk of burns is higher if you cannot feel pain, heat, or cold.

(e)

oO 0

Activity

@ 9

ete

e Return to your normal activities as told by your health care provider. Ask your health care provider what activities are safe for you. e Avoid activities that make your symptoms worse. e Take brief periods of rest throughout the day. o When you rest for longer periods, mix in some mild activity or stretching between periods of rest. This will help to prevent stiffness and pain. o Avoid sitting for long periods of time without moving. Get up and move around at least one time each hour. e Exercise and stretch regularly as told by your health care provider.

Printed on 9/16/25 9:05 AM Page 314




SF oo apristT HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Ruhl, Timothy or 4000 KRESGE WAY MRN: [PHONE-1414], DOB: 8/5/1966, Legal Sex: M Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 8/27/2023, D/C: 8/27/2023

[DOB-1671] - ED in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT (continued)

More Information (continued)

e Do not lift anything that is heavier than 10 Ib (4.5 kg) until your health care provider says that it is safe. When you do not have symptoms, you should still avoid heavy lifting, especially repetitive heavy lifting. e When you lift objects, always use proper lifting technique, which includes: o Bending your knees. o Keeping the load close to your body. o Avoiding twisting.

General instructions

e Maintain a healthy weight. Excess weight puts extra stress on your back.

e Wear supportive, comfortable shoes. Avoid wearing high heels.

e Avoid sleeping on a mattress that is too soft or too hard. A mattress that is firm enough to support your back when you sleep may help to reduce your pain.

Contact a health care provider if:

Your pain is not controlled by medicine. Your pain does not improve or gets worse. Your pain lasts longer than 4 weeks.

You have unexplained weight loss.

Get help right away if:

e You are not able to control when you urinate or have bowel movements (incontinence). e You have:

o Weakness in your lower back, pelvis, buttocks, or legs that gets worse.

o Redness or swelling of your back.

o A burning sensation when you urinate.

Summary

e Sciatica is pain, numbness, weakness, or tingling along the path of the sciatic nerve, which may include the lower back, legs, hips, and buttocks.

e This condition is caused by pressure on the sciatic nerve or pinching of the nerve.

e Treatment often includes rest, exercise, medicines, and applying ice or heat.

This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss any questions you have with your health care provider.

Document Revised: [DOB-1670] Document Reviewed: [DOB-1417] Elsevier Patient Education © 2024 Elsevier Inc.

Call 911 or have someone take you to the Emergency Department if you have any of the following: Sudden numbness or weakness of your face, arm or leg especially on one side of the body Sudden confusion, difficulty speaking or trouble understanding

Changes in your vision or loss of sight in one eye

Sudden severe headache with no known cause

Sudden dizziness, trouble walking, loss of balance or coordination

It is important to seek emergency care right away if you have further stroke symptoms. If you get emergency help quickly, the powerful clot-dissolving medicines can reduce the disabilities caused by a stroke.

For more information: American Stroke Association 1-888-4-STROKE www.strokeassociation.org

IF YOU SMOKE, VAPE OR USE TOBACCO PLEASE READ THE FOLLOWING:

Why is smoking bad for me?

Smoking increases the risk of heart disease, lung disease, vascular disease, stroke, and cancer. If you smoke, STOP!

For more information:

Quit Now Kentucky 1-800-QUIT-NOW https://kentucky.quitlogix.org/en-US/

If you feel like life is too tough and are thinking of suicide or injuring yourself, get help right away! e Call or text 988 to speak to someone.

EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT ENCOUNTER

Room Number: 39/39 Date seen: 4/12/2023 PCP: Marquess, Douglas James, MD

HPI:

Chief Complaint: Strokelike symptoms

A complete HPI/ROS/PMH/PSH/SH/FH are unobtainable due to: None

Context: Timothy Ruhl is a 56 y.o. male who presents to the ED c/o strokelike symptoms. He complains of having left-sided facial droop. Symptoms began gradually yesterday. He cannot smile symmetrically with his left side of his face drooping. He is having difficulty swallowing liquid as dribbles out of his mouth. He cannot close his left eye completely. He is also noticed some abnormal sensation to the dorsum of his left wrist. He is recently diagnosed as a diabetic. He has recently developed cold sores to his lips. He recently noticed that a tick was on him.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY Active Ambulatory Problems

- External (non-ED) record review: On medical chart review, patient saw Dr. Marquis, his internist on [DOB-1553]. Diagnosed with type 2 diabetes. He has acquired hypothyroidism. Patient is reluctant to get COVID vaccines or shingles vaccine.

SF oo apristT HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Ruhl, Timothy or 4000 KRESGE WAY MRN: [PHONE-1554], DOB: 8/5/1966, Legal Sex: M Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 4/12/2023, D/C: 4/12/2023

[DOB-1555] - ED in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT (continued)

ED Provider Note (continued)

Stroke, Bell's palsy, tickborne illness, otitis media

Additional orders considered but not ordered:

| considered obtaining CT imaging to rule out brain bleed as well as CT angiogram to look for cause of stroke. | considered MRI to definitively evaluate for stroke. Patient has symptoms that are very consistent with Bell's palsy but the atypical feature is the abnormal sensation to the dorsum of the left wrist. Therefore, | called neurology and consulted with Dr. Thorton.

Independent interpretation of labs, radiology studies, and discussions with consultants: ED Course as of 04/12/23 2345

Wed Apr 12, 2023

2230 Hemoglobin A1C(!): 7.10 [TD]

2230 ~—_ Lithium: 0.6 [TD]

2230 Creatinine(!): 1.42 [TD]

2230 Sodium: 138 [TD]

2230 Potassium: 4.3 [TD]

2250 eGFR(!): 58.0 [TD]

ED Course User Index [TD] Davis, Thomas Edward II, MD

| called Dr. Thorton, stroke neurology. We reviewed patient's history. Patient's symptoms are rather classic for Bell's palsy given the slowly progressive symptoms over the past 24 hours with associated upper and lower facial symptoms on the left side. He feels that the abnormal sensation to the dorsum of the left wrist is most consistent with a mononeuropathy related to his diabetes rather than a stroke symptom itself. He does not recommend any imaging and instead recommends discharge home with Bell's palsy therapy. | did let the patient know that if he continues to have abnormal symptoms that progressed to his left hand/wrist that he should follow-up with neurology. He is in agreement with this plan.

He has an appointment with his PCP on Thursday.

PPE: The patient wore a mask throughout the entire encounter. | wore a well-fitting mask.

DIAGNOSIS Final diagnoses: Left-sided Bell's palsy

DISPOSITION DISCHARGE

FOLLOW-UP

SF oo apristT HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Ruhl, Timothy or 4000 KRESGE WAY MRN: [PHONE-1642], DOB: 8/5/1966, Legal Sex: M Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 4/12/2023, D/C: 4/12/2023

[DOB-1643] - ED in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT (continued)

More Information

Bell's Palsy in Adults: What to Know

Bell's palsy is a condition that causes short-term weakness or paralysis of the muscles in a part of your face. With paralysis, you're not able to move the affected muscles.

Bell's palsy happens when a nerve in your face, called the seventh cranial nerve, gets irritated, swollen, or has pressure put on it. This nerve runs along your skull, under your ear, and to the side of your face. It controls movements of your face like blinking, closing your eyes, smiling, and frowning.

The symptoms of Bell's palsy may go away on their own in a few weeks. Treatment is sometimes needed.

What are the causes?

The exact cause of Bell's palsy isn't known. It may be caused by irritation and swelling or an infection from a virus, such as the chickenpox (herpes zoster), Epstein-Barr, or mumps virus.

What increases the risk? You're more likely to get Bell's palsy if you:

Are pregnant.

Have diabetes.

Have high blood pressure.

Are obese.

Have recently had an infection in your nose, throat, or upper airways.

Other things that may trigger this condition include:

e A virus that's been in your body but hasn't been active.

e A weakened immune system, which is your body's defense system. It may be weak due to: o Stress. o Not getting enough sleep. o Physical trauma or injury. o A minor illness. o Autoimmune syndromes.

Printed on 9/16/25 9:05 AM Page 370




SF oo apristT HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Ruhl, Timothy or 4000 KRESGE WAY MRN: [PHONE-1644], DOB: 8/5/1966, Legal Sex: M Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 4/12/2023, D/C: 4/12/2023

[DOB-1645] - ED in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT (continued)

More Information (continued)

e Infection or irritation and swelling of the facial nerve. e Damage to the covering of the nerve fibers.

What are the signs or symptoms?

Symptoms of Bell's palsy include:

Sudden weakness on one side of the face.

Drooping of the eyelid, eyebrow, and corner of the mouth. Drooling from one side of the mouth.

Having trouble closing the eyelid.

If you're living with Bell's palsy, you may also develop:

Pain or unusual feelings in your face. A lot of tearing in one eye.

Changes in how things taste.

Being sensitive to sound in one ear. Pain behind one ear.

Pain around one side of the jaw. Trouble eating or drinking.

Most of the time, only one side of the face is affected. In rare cases, Bell's palsy may affect the whole face.

How is this diagnosed?

Diagnosis of Bell's palsy will include a physical exam. Your symptoms and medical history will be checked.

You may also need to see health care providers who are specialists. This may be an expert in nerve disorders called a neurologist or an eye specialist called an ophthalmologist. You may have tests, such as:

e An electromyogram. This test checks for nerve damage. e Imaging tests, such as a CT scan or an MRI. e Blood tests.

How is this treated?

Bell's palsy affects every person differently. Sometimes symptoms go away without treatment within a few weeks. If treatment is needed, it varies from person to person. The goal of treatment is to reduce irritation and swelling and to protect the eye from damage. Treatment for Bell's palsy may include:

Printed on 9/16/25 9:05 AM Page 371




SF oo apristT HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Ruhl, Timothy or 4000 KRESGE WAY MRN: [PHONE-1646], DOB: 8/5/1966, Legal Sex: M Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 4/12/2023, D/C: 4/12/2023

[DOB-1647] - ED in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT (continued)

More Information (continued)

e Medicines, such as: o Steroids to reduce irritation and swelling. o Antiviral medicines. o Medicines for pain, like aspirin, acetaminophen, or ibuprofen. e Eye drops or ointment to keep your eye moist. e Eye protection, if you can't close your eyelid. e Physical therapy exercises to improve movement and strength in your face muscles. This may also include acupuncture or massage.

Follow these instructions at home:

\

\ \

ms

e Take your medicines only as told.

e If your eye is affected: o Keep your eye moist with eye drops or ointment as told by your provider. o Follow your provider's instructions for eye care and protection.

e Do any physical therapy exercises as told.

Where to find more information

e American Academy of Ophthalmology (AAO): aao.org

Contact a health care provider if:

You have a fever or chills.

Your symptoms don't get better within 2-3 weeks, or your symptoms get worse. Your eye is red, irritated, or painful.

You have new symptoms.

You feel light-headed.

Get help right away if:

You have weakness or numbness in a part of your body other than your face. You have trouble swallowing.

You have neck pain or stiffness.

You have shortness of breath.

These symptoms may be an emergency. Call 911 right away.

Printed on 9/16/25 9:05 AM Page 372




SF oo apristT HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Ruhl, Timothy or 4000 KRESGE WAY MRN: [PHONE-1648], DOB: 8/5/1966, Legal Sex: M Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 4/12/2023, D/C: 4/12/2023

[DOB-1649] - ED in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT (continued)

More Information (continued)

e Do not wait to see if the symptoms will go away. e Do not drive yourself to the hospital.

This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss any questions you have with your health care provider.

Document Revised: [DOB-1650] Document Reviewed: [DOB-1651] Elsevier Patient Education © 2024 Elsevier Inc.

Call 911 or have someone take you to the Emergency Department if you have any of the following: Sudden numbness or weakness of your face, arm or leg especially on one side of the body Sudden confusion, difficulty speaking or trouble understanding

Changes in your vision or loss of sight in one eye

Sudden severe headache with no known cause

Sudden dizziness, trouble walking, loss of balance or coordination

It is important to seek emergency care right away if you have further stroke symptoms. If you get emergency help quickly, the powerful clot-dissolving medicines can reduce the disabilities caused by a stroke.

For more information: American Stroke Association 1-888-4-STROKE www.strokeassociation.org

IF YOU SMOKE, VAPE OR USE TOBACCO PLEASE READ THE FOLLOWING:

Why is smoking bad for me?

Smoking increases the risk of heart disease, lung disease, vascular disease, stroke, and cancer. If you smoke, STOP!

For more information:

Quit Now Kentucky 1-800-QUIT-NOW https://kentucky.quitlogix.org/en-US/

Yes XR Spine Lumbar AP & Lateral With Flex & Ext [498150872] Resulted: 07/12/22 1849, Result status: Final result Ordering provider: Fletcher, Billie Jo V, PA 07/12/22 1101 Order status: Completed Resulted by: Rothpletz, John D, MD Filed by: Interface, Rad Results Enterprise In 07/12/22 1852 Performed: 07/12/22 1117 - 07/12/22 1130 Accession number: [PHONE-1757] Resulting lab: BAPTIST HEALTH RADIOLOGY Narrative:

LUMBAR SPINE X-RAYS WITH FLEXION AND EXTENSION VIEWS HISTORY: Back pain. Evaluate degenerative change.

TECHNIQUE: An AP view and four lateral views of the lumbosacral spine including flexion and extension views are provided. No previous imaging of the spine for correlation.

FINDINGS: Vertebral body height and alignment are normal in each position. There is bulky enthesophyte with osseous ankylosis anteriorly across the T11-12, T12-L1, L1-2, and probably the L2-3 disc levels. Disc height is fairly well preserved throughout the lumbar spine, with mild

disc narrowing at L2-3 and L3-4. Facet arthropathy is observed at the lower lumbar levels. The SI joints and upper pelvic bones appear normal. No bone lesion identified.

Impression:

Degenerative change in the lumbosacral spine with multilevel osseous ankylosis across the lower thoracic and upper lumbar levels. There appears to be ankylosis as far caudad as L3. There is facet arthropathy below L3. No static or dynamic subluxation is present.

This report was finalized on 7/12/2022 6:49 PM by Dr. John Rothpletz, M.D.

[DOB-1876] - Transcribe Orders in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE PATIENT ACCESS (continued) Patient Instructions (continued)

None

Call 911 or have someone take you to the Emergency Department if you have any of the following: Sudden numbness or weakness of your face, arm or leg especially on one side of the body Sudden confusion, difficulty speaking or trouble understanding

Changes in your vision or loss of sight in one eye

Sudden severe headache with no known cause

Sudden dizziness, trouble walking, loss of balance or coordination

It is important to seek emergency care right away if you have further stroke symptoms. If you get emergency help quickly, the powerful clot-dissolving medicines can reduce the disabilities caused by a stroke.

For more information: American Stroke Association 1-888-4-STROKE www.strokeassociation.org

IF YOU SMOKE, VAPE OR USE TOBACCO PLEASE READ THE FOLLOWING:

Why is smoking bad for me?

Smoking increases the risk of heart disease, lung disease, vascular disease, stroke, and cancer. If you smoke, STOP!

For more information:

Quit Now Kentucky 1-800-QUIT-NOW https://kentucky.quitlogix.org/en-US/

If you feel like life is too tough and are thinking of suicide or injuring yourself, get help right away! e Call or text 988 to speak to someone.

[DOB-1945] - Ancillary Orders in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE XRAY (continued) Instructions for After Discharge

Patient Instructions None

Call 911 or have someone take you to the Emergency Department if you have any of the following: Sudden numbness or weakness of your face, arm or leg especially on one side of the body Sudden confusion, difficulty speaking or trouble understanding

Changes in your vision or loss of sight in one eye

Sudden severe headache with no known cause

Sudden dizziness, trouble walking, loss of balance or coordination

It is important to seek emergency care right away if you have further stroke symptoms. If you get emergency help quickly, the powerful clot-dissolving medicines can reduce the disabilities caused by a stroke.

For more information: American Stroke Association 1-888-4-STROKE www.strokeassociation.org

[DOB-2016] - Ancillary Orders in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE XRAY (continued) Instructions for After Discharge

Patient Instructions None

Call 911 or have someone take you to the Emergency Department if you have any of the following: Sudden numbness or weakness of your face, arm or leg especially on one side of the body Sudden confusion, difficulty speaking or trouble understanding

Changes in your vision or loss of sight in one eye

Sudden severe headache with no known cause

Sudden dizziness, trouble walking, loss of balance or coordination

It is important to seek emergency care right away if you have further stroke symptoms. If you get emergency help quickly, the powerful clot-dissolving medicines can reduce the disabilities caused by a stroke.

For more information: American Stroke Association 1-888-4-STROKE

Chief Complaint Patient presents with * Diarrhea

Subjective HPI Timothy Ruhl is a 55 y.o. male who presents today for new patient evaluation

Chronic cough - fits to point where he can pass out. Seems to be worse with wearing mask. Hx of COPD. Hasnt seen pulmonologist for his coughing issue. No typical GERD. Tried PPI but no improvement. No prior EGD. No prior colonoscopy.

LOUISVILLE Patient Name: Timothy Ruhl Procedure Date: 11/3/2021 2:12 PM MRN: [PHONE-2164] Account Number: 91116214270 Date of Birth: 8/5/1966 Admit Type: Outpatient Age: 55 Gender: Male Attending MD: Brian David Beauerle, MD

Name of Procedure: | Upper GI endoscopy Procedure Performed By: Brian David Beauerle, MD, Courtney Richelle Protzer, MD (Anesthesia Staff) Referring MD: Brian David Beauerle, MD (Referring MD), Douglas Marquess (Referring MD) Indications: Chronic cough Medications: Propofol per Anesthesia Complications: No immediate complications. Procedure: After obtaining informed consent, the endoscope was passed under direct vision. Throughout the procedure, the patient's blood pressure, pulse, and oxygen saturations were monitored continuously. The Endoscope was introduced through the mouth, and advanced to the third part of duodenum. The upper GI endoscopy was accomplished without difficulty. The patient tolerated the procedure well.

Upper Gastrointestinal Tract

Findings: - LA Grade A (one or more mucosal breaks less than 5 mm, not extending between tops of 2 mucosal folds) esophagitis with no bleeding was found at the gastroesophageal junction. Biopsies were taken with a cold forceps for histology.

- Patchy minimal inflammation characterized by erythema was found in the gastric antrum. Biopsies were taken with a cold forceps for histology. - The examined duodenum was normal.

Add'l Images:

EB 2nd Portion of the Duodenum

Lower Third of the Esophagus

LOUISVILLE Patient Name: Timothy Ruhl Procedure Date: 11/3/2021 2:12 PM MRN: [PHONE-2174] Account Number: 91116214270 Date of Birth: 8/5/1966 Admit Type: Outpatient Age: 55 Gender: Male Attending MD: Brian David Beauerle, MD

Name of Procedure: Colonoscopy

Procedure Performed By: Brian David Beauerle, MD, Brittany Porter, CRNA (Anesthesia Staff) Referring MD: Brian David Beauerle, MD (Referring MD), Douglas Marquess (Referring MD) Indications: Screening for colorectal malignant neoplasm

Medications: Propofol per Anesthesia

Complications: No immediate complications.

Procedure:

After | obtained informed consent, the scope was passed under direct vision. Throughout the procedure, the patient's blood pressure, pulse, and oxygen saturations were monitored continuously. The Colonoscope was introduced through the anus and advanced to the cecum, identified by appendiceal orifice and ileocecal valve. The colonoscopy was performed without difficulty. The patient tolerated the procedure well. The quality of the bowel preparation was adequate to identify

polyps.

The Colon Findings: - The perianal and digital rectal examinations were normal. - The entire examined colon appeared normal on direct and retroflexion views.

Add'l Images:

Cecum Transverse Colon Descending Colon

(5}) Sigmoid Colon | 6 | Rectum

Impression:

- The entire examined colon is normal on direct and retroflexion views. - No specimens collected.

LOUISVILLE Patient Name: Timothy Ruhl Procedure Date: 11/3/2021 2:12 PM MRN: [PHONE-2179] Account Number: 91116214270 Date of Birth: 8/5/1966 Admit Type: Outpatient Age: 55 Gender: Male Attending MD: Brian David Beauerle, MD

Recommendation:

- Repeat colonoscopy in 10 years for screening purposes. - Please know that small polyps can be missed based on many factors, including the quality of the bowel prep, anatomy, and motility of the colon during the procedure. These polyps could cause problems (including cancer) before your next colonoscopy is due. Please let your physician know if you develop any problems, such as blood in your stool, change in your stool habits, abdominal pain or other concerning changes.

Number of Addenda:

Note Initiated On:

Estimated Blood Loss: Estimated blood loss: none.

Scope Withdrawal Time: 7 Minutes 30 Seconds

Total Procedure Duration Time: 12 Minutes 22 Seconds

Diagnosis Code(s): --- Professional --- 212.11, Encounter for screening for malignant neoplasm of colon

LOUISVILLE Patient Name: Timothy Ruhl Procedure Date: 11/3/2021 2:12 PM MRN: [PHONE-2194] Account Number: 91116214270 Date of Birth: 8/5/1966 Admit Type: Outpatient Age: 55 Gender: Male Attending MD: Brian David Beauerle, MD

Name of Procedure: Colonoscopy

Procedure Performed By: Brian David Beauerle, MD, Brittany Porter, CRNA (Anesthesia Staff) Referring MD: Brian David Beauerle, MD (Referring MD), Douglas Marquess (Referring MD) Indications: Screening for colorectal malignant neoplasm

Medications: Propofol per Anesthesia

Complications: No immediate complications.

Procedure:

After | obtained informed consent, the scope was passed under direct vision. Throughout the procedure, the patient's blood pressure, pulse, and oxygen saturations were monitored continuously. The Colonoscope was introduced through the anus and advanced to the cecum, identified by appendiceal orifice and ileocecal valve. The colonoscopy was performed without difficulty. The patient tolerated the procedure well. The quality of the bowel preparation was adequate to identify

polyps.

The Colon Findings: - The perianal and digital rectal examinations were normal. - The entire examined colon appeared normal on direct and retroflexion views.

Add'l Images:

Cecum Transverse Colon Descending Colon

(5}) Sigmoid Colon | 6 | Rectum

Impression:

- The entire examined colon is normal on direct and retroflexion views. - No specimens collected.

Ba ° oy BAPTIST HEALTH

LOUISVILLE Patient Name: Timothy Ruhl Procedure Date: 11/3/2021 2:12 PM MRN: [PHONE-2197] Account Number: 91116214270 Date of Birth: 8/5/1966 Admit Type: Outpatient Age: 55 Gender: Male Attending MD: Brian David Beauerle, MD

Recommendation:

- Repeat colonoscopy in 10 years for screening purposes. - Please know that small polyps can be missed based on many factors, including the quality of the bowel prep, anatomy, and motility of the colon during the procedure. These polyps could cause problems (including cancer) before your next colonoscopy is due. Please let your physician know if you develop any problems, such as blood in your stool, change in your stool habits, abdominal pain or other concerning changes.

Care Plan Active Problem: Adult Inpatient Plan of Care Disciplines: Nurse, Interdisciplinary Goal: Plan of Care Review Dates: Start: 11/03/21 Disciplines: Nurse, Interdisciplinary

Goal: Patient-Specific Goal (Individualized)

Dates: Start: 11/03/21 Disciplines: Nurse, Interdisciplinary

Goal: Absence of Hospital-Acquired Illness or Injury

Disciplines: Nurse, Interdisciplinary Intervention: Identify and Manage Fall Risk

Frequency: Description:

Per CPG

Perform standard risk assessment with a validated tool or comprehensive approach appropriate to the patient on admission; reassess fall risk frequently, with change in status or transfer to another level of care.

Communicate fall injury risk to interprofessional healthcare team.

Determine need for increased observation, equipment and environmental modification, such as low bed and signage, as well as supportive, nonskid footwear.

Adjust safety measures to individual developmental age, stage and identified risk factors. Reinforce the importance of safety and physical activity with patient and family.

Perform regular intentional rounding to assess need for position change, pain assessment, personal needs, including assistance with toileting.

Intervention: Prevent Skin Injury

Frequency: Description:

Printed on 9/16/25 9:06 AM

Per CPG

Assess skin risk on admission and at regular intervals throughout hospital stay.

Keep all areas of skin (especially folds) clean and dry.

Maintain adequate skin hydration.

Relieve and redistribute pressure and protect bony prominences; implement measures based on patient-specific risk factors.

Match turning and repositioning schedule to clinical condition.

Encourage weight shift frequently; assist with reposition if unable to complete independently. Float heels off bed. Avoid pressure on the Achilles tendon.

Keep skin free from extended contact with medical devices.

Use aids (e.g., slide boards, mechanical lift) during transfer.

Baptist Health Louisville Ruhl, Timothy 4000 KRESGE WAY MRN: [PHONE-2270], DOB: 8/5/1966, Legal Sex: M Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 11/3/2021, D/C: 11/3/2021

[DOB-2271] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE ENDO SUITES (continued)

Care Plan (continued)

Intervention: Prevent and Manage VTE (venous thromboembolism) Risk

Frequency: Description:

Per CPG

Assess for VTE risk.

Encourage/assist with early ambulation.

Initiate and maintain compression or other therapy, as indicated based on identified risk in accordance with organizational protocol/provider order.

Encourage both active and passive leg exercises while in bed, if unable to ambulate.

Intervention: Prevent Infection

Frequency: Description:

Per CPG

Maintain skin and mucous membrane integrity; promote hand, oral and pulmonary hygiene. Optimize fluid balance, nutrition, sleep and glycemic control to maximize infection resistance. Identify potential sources of infection early to prevent or mitigate progression of infection (e.g., wound, lines, devices).

Evaluate ongoing need for invasive devices; remove promptly when no longer indicated.

Goal: Optimal Comfort and Wellbeing

Disciplines:

Nurse, Interdisciplinary

Intervention: Monitor Pain and Promote Comfort

Frequency: Description:

Per CPG

Assess pain level, treatment efficacy and patient response at regular intervals using a consistent pain scale.

Consider the presence and impact of preexisting chronic pain.

Encourage patient and caregiver involvement in pain assessment, interventions and safety measures.

Intervention: Provide Person-Centered Care

Frequency: Description:

Per CPG

Use a family-focused approach to care.

Develop trust and rapport by proactively providing information, encouraging questions, addressing concerns and offering reassurance.

Acknowledge emotional response to hospitalization.

Recognize and utilize personal coping strategies.

Honor spiritual and cultural preferences.

Goal: Readiness for Transition of Care

Disciplines:

Nurse, Interdisciplinary

Intervention: Mutually Develop Transition Plan

Frequency: Description:

Coding Queries

Per CPG

Identify available resources for support (e.g., family, friends, community).

Identify and address barriers to ongoing treatment and home management (e.g., environmental, financial).

Provide opportunities to practice self-management skills.

Assess and monitor emotional readiness for transition.

Establish/reconnect linkage with outpatient providers or community-based services.

Minor ortemporary reactions, such as slight bruising, swelling, orloc al infection of the area where the needle pierces the skinis not uncommon. Non-serious reactions may include headache, fever, or a mild skin reaction, such as itching or arash. Rare, but serious reactions are possible such as the transmission of diseas es including hepatitis and AIDS, which require lengthy treatment and can be fatal. record. ANESTHESIA

| understand that this procedure may require anesthesia. An anesthesiology provider has or will explain the potential benefits, risks, side effects, and potential anesthesia alternatives. If your provider recommends and will manage local anesthesia or moderate sedation as noted below, this discussion will occur with your provider. Complications from any type of anesthetic are infrequent, but do occur. Complications are more frequent for patients in poor health than for patients in good health. Complications associated with all types of anesthesia include temporary or permanent damage to teeth, lips, nose, throat, voice box, windpipe, eyes, nerves, or blood vessels. Drug reaction may occur and may be minor or serious even requiring resuscitation. Breathing problems may develop. Headache, back pain, some degree of awareness, infection, nausea and/or | vomiting may occur. There are some very serious complications, which are uncommon, but can occur. These include paralysis, brain damage, or death. There are other potential complications depending on your situation, however, it is not possible to advise you of every imaginable complication. Serious complications are unlikely. The benefits of surgery and anesthesia, except in extreme circumstances, outweigh the potential risks. | agree that the potential benefit of the procedure(s) outweighs the potential risks of anesthesia.

| understand that my procedure is scheduled to be done with one or more of the following:

General Anesthesia Monitoring and care provided by the anesthesiologist, a Certified Registered Nurse Anesthetist [CRNA], or an Anesthesiologist's Assistant [AA].

Monitored Anesthesia Care Possible sedation, monitoring and care provided by the anesthesiologist, a CRNA, or an AA.

Regional Anesthesia Monitoring and care provided by the anesthesiologist, CRNA, oran AA.

Local Anesthesia Care managed by the Provider and monitored by a registered nurse.

Moderate Sedation Care managed by the Provider and monitored by a registered nurse.

There is the possibility that any of the services provided above may convert to a general anesthetic. These same complications may occur with local anesthesia. More Information

DR BEAUERLE 893-0220 Colonoscopy, Adult, Care After

This sheet gives you information about how to care for yourself after your procedure. Your health care provider may also give you more specific instructions. If you have problems or questions, contact your health care provider.

What can I expect after the procedure?

After the procedure, it is common to have:

e A small amount of blood in your stool for 24 hours after the procedure. e Some gas. e Mild cramping or bloating of your abdomen.

Follow these instructions at home:

Eating and drinking e Drink enough fluid to keep your urine pale yellow. CAEL CAT e Follow instructions from your health care provider about eating or drinking restrictions. | oe ios e Resume your normal diet as instructed by your health care provider. Avoid

heavy or fried foods that are hard to digest.

Activity

e Rest as told by your health care provider.

e Avoid sitting for a long time without moving. Get up to take short walks every 1—2 hours. This is important to improve blood flow and breathing. Ask for help if you feel weak or unsteady.

e Return to your normal activities as told by your health care provider. Ask your health care provider what activities are safe for you.

Managing cramping and bloating

e Try walking around when you have cramps or feel bloated. e Apply heat to your abdomen as told by your health care provider. Use the heat source —_— that your health care provider recommends, such as a moist heat pack or a heating pad. OF eee o Place a towel between your skin and the heat source. 1 ———— oO Leave the heat on for 20—30 minutes. o Remove the heat if your skin turns bright red. This is especially important if you are unable to feel pain, heat, or cold. You may have a greater risk of getting burned.

Printed on 9/16/25 9:06 AM Page 514




SF oo apristT HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Ruhl, Timothy or 4000 KRESGE WAY MRN: [PHONE-2300], DOB: 8/5/1966, Legal Sex: M Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 11/3/2021, D/C: 11/3/2021

[DOB-2301] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE ENDO SUITES (continued)

More Information (continued)

General instructions

e Ifyou were given a sedative during the procedure, it can affect you for several hours. Do not drive or operate machinery until your health care provider says that it is safe. eFor the first 24 hours after the procedure: oO Do not sign important documents. © Do not drink alcohol. oO Do your regular daily activities at a slower pace than normal. o Eat soft foods that are easy to digest. e Take over-the-counter and prescription medicines only as told by your health care provider. e Keep all follow-up visits as told by your health care provider. This is important.

Contact a health care provider if: e You have blood in your stool 2—3 days after the procedure. Get help right away if you have:

More than a small spotting of blood in your stool.

Large blood clots in your stool.

Swelling of your abdomen.

Nausea or vomiting.

A fever.

Increasing pain in your abdomen that is not relieved with medicine.

Summary

e After the procedure, it is common to have a small amount of blood in your stool. You may also have mild cramping and bloating of your abdomen.

e Ifyou were given a sedative during the procedure, it can affect you for several hours. Do not drive or operate machinery until your health care provider says that it is safe.

e Get help right away if you have a lot of blood in your stool, nausea or vomiting, a fever, or increased pain in your abdomen.

This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss any questions you have with your health care provider.

Upper Endoscopy, Adult, Care After

After the procedure, it is common to have a sore throat. It is also common to have:

e Mild stomach pain or discomfort. e Bloating. e Nausea.

Follow these instructions at home:

The instructions below may help you care for yourself at home. Your health care provider may give you more instructions. If you have questions, ask your health care provider.

e If you were given a sedative during the procedure, it can affect you for several hours. Do not drive or operate machinery until your health care provider says that it is safe. e If you will be going home right after the procedure, plan to have a responsible adult: o Take you home from the hospital or clinic. You will not be allowed to drive. o Care for you for the time you are told. e Follow instructions from your health care provider about what you may eat and drink. e Return to your normal activities as told by your health care provider. Ask your health care provider what activities are safe for you. e Take over-the-counter and prescription medicines only as told by your health care provider.

Contact a health care provider if you:

e Have a sore throat that lasts longer than one day. e Have trouble swallowing. e Have a fever.

Get help right away if you:

Vomit blood or your vomit looks like coffee grounds.

Have bloody, black, or tarry stools.

Have a very bad sore throat or you cannot swallow.

Have difficulty breathing or very bad pain in your chest or abdomen.

These symptoms may be an emergency. Get help right away. Call 911.

e Do not wait to see if the symptoms will go away. e Do not drive yourself to the hospital.

Summary

e After the procedure, it is common to have a sore throat, mild stomach discomfort, bloating, and nausea.

e lf you were given a sedative during the procedure, it can affect you for several hours. Do not drive until your health care provider says that it is safe.

e Follow instructions from your health care provider about what you may eat and drink. e Return to your normal activities as told by your health care provider.

This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss any questions you have with your health care provider.

Irritation and Swelling of the Esophagus (Esophagitis): What to Know

Esophagus

Stomach >>

Esophagitis is irritation and swelling of the esophagus. The esophagus is the part of your body that moves food from your mouth to your stomach.

Esophagitis can cause soreness or pain in your esophagus. It can make it hard or painful to swallow.

What are the causes? Most causes of esophagitis aren't serious. Common causes include:

e Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). This is when stomach contents move back up into your esophagus. e An allergic reaction. This may be caused by food allergies. e An injury to your esophagus. This may happen if: o You swallow large pills. o You swallow certain medicines. o You swallow harmful chemicals. These include cleaning products. o You throw up a lot. Drinking a lot of alcohol or smoking. An infection. This may happen if your body has a weak defense system (immune system). Certain treatments for cancer. Certain diseases. These include: o Sarcoidosis. o Crohn's disease. o Scleroderma.

What are the signs or symptoms?

e Trouble swallowing.

Printed on 9/16/25 9:06 AM Page 518




SF oo apristT HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Ruhl, Timothy or 4000 KRESGE WAY MRN: [PHONE-2312], DOB: 8/5/1966, Legal Sex: M Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 11/3/2021, D/C: 11/3/2021

[DOB-2313] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE ENDO SUITES (continued)

More Information (continued)

e Pain when you swallow. This may include when you swallow acidic liquids, such as orange juice. You may also have pain when you burp.

Chest pain or trouble breathing.

Throwing up or feeling like you may throw up.

Pain in your belly.

Weight loss.

Throwing up blood.

Poop that is black, tarry, or bright red.

How is this diagnosed?

Esophagitis may be diagnosed based on your medical history and an exam. You may also have other tests. These may include:

e An endoscopy. This test looks at your stomach and esophagus with a small camera. e Tests of your esophagus to check for: © Acid levels. o Pressure. e A barium swallow test or modified barium swallow test. This can show: o The shape and size of your esophagus. o How well your esophagus is working. e Allergy tests.

How is this treated?

Treatment depends on what is causing the esophagitis. You may be given medicines to help with symptoms. You may also need to:

Avoid alcohol.

Quit smoking.

Change your diet.

Exercise.

Stay at a healthy weight.

Change your sleep habits and how you sleep.

Follow these instructions at home: Medicines

e Take your medicines only as told.

e Do not take aspirin or ibuprofen unless you're told to.

e If you have trouble taking pills: o Use a pill splitter. This can lower the chance of the pill getting stuck or hurting your esophagus. o Drink water after you take a pill.

Eating and drinking

Printed on 9/16/25 9:06 AM Page 519




SF oo apristT HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Ruhl, Timothy or 4000 KRESGE WAY MRN: [PHONE-2314], DOB: 8/5/1966, Legal Sex: M Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 11/3/2021, D/C: 11/3/2021

[DOB-2315] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE ENDO SUITES (continued)

More Information (continued)

e Stay away from foods and drinks that seem to make your symptoms worse. e Eat and drink as told by your health care provider. You may need to avoid certain foods and drinks. These may include: o Coffee and tea, with or without caffeine. Energy drinks and sports drinks. Fizzy drinks or sodas. Chocolate and cocoa. Peppermint and mint flavorings. Garlic and onions. Spicy and acidic foods. These include: = Peppers. = Horseradish. " Chili powder and curry powder. « Vinegar. =" Hot sauces and BBQ sauce. o Citrus fruits and juices. These include: =" Oranges. =» Lemons. = Limes. o Tomato-based foods. These include: =" Red sauce and pizza with red sauce. * Chili. " Salsa. o Fried and fatty foods. These include: =" Donuts. " French fries. " Potato chips. « High-fat dressings. o High-fat meats. These include: = Hot dogs and sausages. = Ribeye steak. =» Ham and bacon. o High-fat dairy items. These include: =" Whole milk. = Butter. =" Cream cheese.

oO0O00 0 0

Lifestyle

Eat small meals often. Avoid eating big meals.

Avoid drinking lots of liquid with your meals.

Try not to eat meals during the 2—3 hours before bedtime. Try not to lie down right after you eat.

Do not exercise right after you eat.

Do not smoke, vape, or use nicotine or tobacco.

General instructions

Printed on 9/16/25 9:06 AM Page 520




SF oo apristT HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Ruhl, Timothy or 4000 KRESGE WAY MRN: [PHONE-2316], DOB: 8/5/1966, Legal Sex: M Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 11/3/2021, D/C: 11/3/2021

[DOB-2317] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE ENDO SUITES (continued)

More Information (continued)

Watch for any changes in your symptoms.

Wear loose clothes. Do not wear things that are tight around your waist.

Raise the head of your bed about 6 inches (15 cm). You can use a wedge to do this.

Try yoga, deep breathing, or meditation to help manage stress.

If you're overweight, lose an amount of weight that's healthy for you. Ask your provider about a safe weight loss goal.

Contact a health care provider if:

Your symptoms don't get better with treatment. You have new symptoms. You lose weight and don't know why. You have trouble swallowing, or it hurts to swallow. You have a cough, or you start wheezing. o This is when you make high-pitched whistling sounds when you breathe, most often when you breathe out. e You have heartburn for more than 2 weeks. e You have a fever.

Get help right away if:

You have very bad pain in your arms, neck, jaw, teeth, or back. You feel sweaty, dizzy, or light-headed all of a sudden. You have chest pain or feel short of breath. You throw up and: o It's green, yellow, or black. o It looks like blood or coffee grounds. e Your poop is red, bloody, or black. e You can't swallow, drink, or eat.

These symptoms may be an emergency. Call 911 right away.

e Do not wait to see if the symptoms will go away. e Do not drive yourself to the hospital.

This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss any questions you have with your health care provider.

Gastritis, Adult

Inflammation Ulcer

Gastritis is inflammation of the stomach. There are two kinds of gastritis:

e Acute gastritis. This kind develops suddenly. e Chronic gastritis. This kind is much more common. It develops slowly and lasts for a long time.

Gastritis happens when the lining of the stomach becomes weak or gets damaged. Without treatment, gastritis can lead to stomach bleeding and ulcers.

What are the causes?

This condition may be caused by:

e An infection.

e Drinking too much alcohol.

e Certain medicines. These include steroids, antibiotics, and some over-the-counter medicines, such as aspirin or ibuprofen.

e Having too much acid in the stomach.

e Having a disease of the stomach.

Other causes may include:

e An allergic reaction. e Some cancer treatments (radiation). e Smoking cigarettes or the use of products that contain nicotine or tobacco.

In some cases, the cause of this condition is not known. What increases the risk?

e Having a disease of the intestines.

e Having a disease in which the body's immune system attacks the body (autoimmune disease), such as Crohn's disease.

e Using aspirin or ibuprofen and other NSAIDs to treat other conditions, such as heart disease or chronic pain.

e Stress.

What are the signs or symptoms?

Printed on 9/16/25 9:06 AM Page 522




SF oo apristT HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Ruhl, Timothy or 4000 KRESGE WAY MRN: [PHONE-2322], DOB: 8/5/1966, Legal Sex: M Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 11/3/2021, D/C: 11/3/2021

[DOB-2323] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE ENDO SUITES (continued)

More Information (continued)

Symptoms of this condition include:

Pain or a burning sensation in the upper abdomen. Nausea.

Vomiting.

An uncomfortable feeling of fullness after eating. Weight loss.

Bad breath.

Blood in your vomit or stool (feces).

In some cases, there are no symptoms.

How is this diagnosed?

This condition may be diagnosed based on your medical history, a physical exam, and tests. Tests may include:

e Your medical history and a description of your symptoms. e A physical exam. e Tests. These can include: o Blood tests. °o Stool tests. o A test in which a thin, flexible instrument with a light and a camera is passed down the esophagus and into the stomach (upper endoscopy). o A test in which a tissue sample is removed to look at it under a microscope (biopsy).

How is this treated?

This condition may be treated with medicines. The medicines that are used vary depending on the cause of the gastritis.

e lf the condition is caused by a bacterial infection, you may be given antibiotic medicines. e lf the condition is caused by too much acid in the stomach, you may be given medicines called H2 blockers, proton pump inhibitors, or antacids.

Treatment may also involve stopping the use of certain medicines such as aspirin or ibuprofen and other NSAIDs.

Follow these instructions at home: Medicines

e Take over-the-counter and prescription medicines only as told by your health care provider. e lf you were prescribed an antibiotic medicine, take it as told by your health care provider. Do not stop taking the antibiotic even if you start to feel better.

Alcohol use

Printed on 9/16/25 9:06 AM Page 523




SF oo apristT HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Ruhl, Timothy or 4000 KRESGE WAY MRN: [PHONE-2324], DOB: 8/5/1966, Legal Sex: M Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 11/3/2021, D/C: 11/3/2021

[DOB-2325] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE ENDO SUITES (continued)

More Information (continued)

e Do not drink alcohol if: o Your health care provider tells you not to drink. o You are pregnant, may be pregnant, or are planning to become pregnant. e If you drink alcohol: o Limit your use to: = 0-1 drink a day for women. = 0-2 drinks a day for men. o Know how much alcohol is in your drink. In the U.S., one drink equals one 12 oz bottle of beer (355 mL), one 5 oz glass of wine (148 mL), or one 1’ oz glass of hard liquor (44 mL).

General instructions

Se =

e Eat small, frequent meals instead of large meals.

e Avoid foods and drinks that make your symptoms worse.

e Talk with your health care provider about ways to manage stress, such as getting regular exercise or practicing deep breathing, meditation, or yoga.

e Do not use any products that contain nicotine or tobacco. These products include cigarettes, chewing tobacco, and vaping devices, such as e-cigarettes. If you need help quitting, ask your health care provider.

e Drink enough fluid to keep your urine pale yellow.

e Keep all follow-up visits. This is important.

Contact a health care provider if:

e Your symptoms get worse.

e Your abdominal pain gets worse.

e Your symptoms return after treatment. e You have a fever.

Get help right away if:

e You vomit blood or a substance that looks like coffee grounds. e You have black or dark red stools. e You are unable to keep fluids down.

These symptoms may represent a serious problem that is an emergency. Do not wait to see if the symptoms will go away. Get medical help right away. Call your local emergency services (911 in the U.S.). Do not drive yourself to the hospital.

Summary Printed on 9/16/25 9:06 AM Page 524

e Gastritis is inflammation of the lining of the stomach that can occur suddenly (acute) or develop slowly over time (chronic).

e This condition is diagnosed with a medical history, a physical exam, or tests.

e This condition may be treated with medicines to treat infection or medicines to reduce the amount of acid in your stomach.

e Follow your health care provider's instructions about taking medicines, making changes to your diet, and knowing when to call for help.

This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss any questions you have with your health care provider.

Call 911 or have someone take you to the Emergency Department if you have any of the following: Sudden numbness or weakness of your face, arm or leg especially on one side of the body Sudden confusion, difficulty speaking or trouble understanding

Changes in your vision or loss of sight in one eye

Sudden severe headache with no known cause

Sudden dizziness, trouble walking, loss of balance or coordination

It is important to seek emergency care right away if you have further stroke symptoms. If you get emergency help quickly, the powerful clot-dissolving medicines can reduce the disabilities caused by a stroke.

For more information: American Stroke Association 1-888-4-STROKE www.strokeassociation.org

IF YOU SMOKE, VAPE OR USE TOBACCO PLEASE READ THE FOLLOWING:

Why is smoking bad for me?

Smoking increases the risk of heart disease, lung disease, vascular disease, stroke, and cancer. If you smoke, STOP!

For more information:

Quit Now Kentucky 1-800-QUIT-NOW https://kentucky.quitlogix.org/en-US/

If you feel like life is too tough and are thinking of suicide or injuring yourself, get help right away! e Call or text 988 to speak to someone.

00 NA rent ooean MRN: [PHONE-2424] 400018 DOB: 8/5/1966 AGE:55 y.o. BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE ("Hospital") HAR: 400003450496 SEX: Male INPATIENT AND OUTPATIENT CONDITIONS AND CSN: 91117516924 ADMIT DATE: CONSENTS IANA

1. CONSENT TO DIAGNOSTIC TESTS, PROCEDURES, & MEDICALTREATMENT: | voluntarily consent to (and consent on behalf of any child born to me while hospitalized) care involving routine diagnostic tests, procedures and medical treatment as ordered by my admitting and treating physicians, including their assistants or designees. | consent to receive medical care through the use of telehealth and/or remote patient monitoring. | further consent to the interpretation of diagnostic studies from an off-site location using telehealth technologies. | consent to medical, nursing, allied health and other students observing and participating in my care under the supervision of a qualified professional. | consent to photographic recordings or reproducible images during the surgical, medical, and/or diagnostic procedure(s) and their use for identification, scientific, educational, or research purposes. | also consent to testing for communicable and blood-borne infectious diseases, such as hepatitis, tuberculosis, and the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV), if a provider orders testing for diagnostic purposes or if there has been an exposure to healthcare personnel. | have been given no guarantees about the results that may be obtained from my care. | understand and agree that the results of diagnostic tests, procedures and medical treatment ordered and provided by providers while | am hospitalized, including a notice of admission to the hospital will be provided to my primary care physician on record, if any.

2. PHYSICIANS AND OTHER HEALTH CARE PROVIDERS WHO ARE NOT EMPLOYEES OF THE HOSPITAL: Physicians and certified registered nurse anesthetists rendering medical treatment to patients in the hospital are not employed by the Hospital. In most situations, advanced practitioners, including but not limited to, nurse practitioners, physician assistants, surgical assistants and psychologists, providing treatment to patients in the Hospital are not employed by the Hospital. You may receive a separate bill for the services of these providers, or the Hospital bill you receive may have separate charges for services of such providers. Charges for such services are established by the providers.

3. FINANCIAL AGREEMENT AND ASSIGNMENT OF BENEFITS: | agree that | am responsible for payment of my Hospital bill. Payment of any portion of my bill not covered by a third party payor is due upon discharge from the Hospital unless the Hospital has agreed to other arrangements. | agree to the assignment of all third party payor benefits to the Hospital and to any health care provider rendering services to me. | acknowledge and agree that Hospital is not required to accept assignment of any third party payor benefits, in which case, | may receive a bill from Hospital for the full amount of charges related to any care or treatment provided to me or my guarantee by Hospital and | agree to pay the Hospital and other health care providers for such charges. Moreover, | understand that Hospital may accept payment from third party payors with whom it does not have a written signed agreement and that such acceptance of payment does not constitute acceptance by Hospital of any reimbursement rates established by such third party payors and that | may receive a bill from Hospital for the difference between the rate paid by such third party payors and the Hospital’s charges. | agree to pay the Hospital and other health care providers for all charges for services that are not covered or paid by any third party payor regardless of the reason, including but not limited to a determination by any third party payor that such services are not covered services or medically necessary. To the extent | am a Medicare or Medicaid beneficiary, | certify that any information | provide in applying for payment under Title XVIII (Medicare) or Title XIX (Medicaid) of the Social Security Act is correct. | request payment of authorized benefits to be made on my behalf to Baptist Health facility or to any health care provider rendering services to me by the Medicare or Medicaid program. | hereby irrevocably appoint Hospital as my authorized representative to pursue any claims, penalties, and administrative and/or legal remedies on my behalf for collection against any responsible payer, employer-sponsored medical benefit plans, third party liability carrier or, any other responsible third party for any and all benefits due me for the payment of charges associated with my treatment. None

Call 911 or have someone take you to the Emergency Department if you have any of the following: Sudden numbness or weakness of your face, arm or leg especially on one side of the body Sudden confusion, difficulty speaking or trouble understanding

Changes in your vision or loss of sight in one eye

Sudden severe headache with no known cause

Sudden dizziness, trouble walking, loss of balance or coordination

It is important to seek emergency care right away if you have further stroke symptoms. If you get emergency help quickly, the powerful clot-dissolving medicines can reduce the disabilities caused by a stroke.

For more information: American Stroke Association 1-888-4-STROKE www.strokeassociation.org

IF YOU SMOKE, VAPE OR USE TOBACCO PLEASE READ THE FOLLOWING:

Why is smoking bad for me?

Smoking increases the risk of heart disease, lung disease, vascular disease, stroke, and cancer. If you smoke, STOP!

For more information:

Quit Now Kentucky 1-800-QUIT-NOW https://kentucky.quitlogix.org/en-US/

If you feel like life is too tough and are thinking of suicide or injuring yourself, get help right away! e Call or text 988 to speak to someone.

Result Findings Left Ventricle Calculated left ventricular EF = 58% Estimated left ventricular EF was in agreement with the calculated left ventricular EF. Left ventricular systolic function is normal. Normal left ventricular cavity size noted. Left ventricular wall thickness is consistent with mild to moderate concentric hypertrophy. All left ventricular wall segments contract normally. Left ventricular diastolic function is consistent with (grade 1) impaired relaxation.

Right Ventricle Normal right ventricular cavity size and systolic function noted.

Left Atrium Normal left atrial cavity size noted. Saline test results are negative.

Right Atrium Normal right atrial cavity size noted.

Mitral Valve The mitral valve is grossly normal in structure. No significant mitral valve regurgitation is present. No significant mitral valve stenosis is present.

Tricuspid Valve No significant tricuspid valve regurgitation or significant stenosis present. Insufficient TR velocity profile to estimate the right ventricular systolic pressure.

Aortic Valve The aortic valve is structurally normal with no stenosis present. No significant aortic valve regurgitation is present.

Pulmonic Valve The pulmonic valve is not well visualized. The pulmonic valve is grossly normal in structure.

Pericardium There is no evidence of pericardial effusion. .

Additional Study Details

A complete transthoracic echocardiogram with complete Doppler and color flow was performed. The study is technically adequate for diagnosis. Verbal consent was obtained for use of agitated saline to assess for shunting. A total of 20 mL of agitated saline was administered.

Greater Vessels No dilation of the aortic root is present. The inferior vena cava is normally sized. Normal IVC inspiratory collapse of greater than 50% noted.

EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT ENCOUNTER

Room Number: 23/23

Date of encounter: 4/27/2021

PCP: Marquess, Douglas James, MD Historian: Patient

HPI: Chief Complaint: Lower extremity edema A complete HPI/ROS/PMH/PSH/SH/FH are unobtainable due to: N/A

Context: Timothy Ruhl is a 54 y.o. male who presents to the ED c/o lower extremity edema. He was seen by his primary care doctor recently and was put on Lasix for lower extremity edema. Edema resolved, so he stopped his Lasix 3 to 4 days ago at the instruction of his primary care physician per his report. This morning, he noticed his legs were swollen. He took 20 mg of Lasix this afternoon (which was the last pill) and has not noticed any relief. He does have mild tenderness. He has chronic cough and congestion shortness of breath-he states this is no worse than usual. He denies chest pain. No nausea, vomiting or diarrhea. Of note, he has not been vaccinated for COVID-19 and has no plans to. He also refused to wear a mask in the room.

The patient was placed in a mask in triage, hand hygiene was performed before and after my interaction with the patient. | wore a mask, safety glasses and gloves during my entire interaction with the patient.

Constitutional: Pt. is oriented to person, place, and time and well-developed, well-nourished, and in no distress. No distress.

HENT: Normocephalic and atraumatic, EOM are normal. Pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light. Oropharynx moist/nonerythematous.

Neck: Normal range of motion. Neck supple. No JVD present. No tracheal deviation present. No thyromegaly present. Cardiovascular: Normal rate, regular rhythm and normal heart sounds. Exam reveals no gallop and no friction rub. No murmur heard.

Pulmonary/Chest: Effort normal and breath sounds normal. No stridor. No respiratory distress. No wheezes, no rales. Abdominal: Soft. Bowel sounds are normal. No distension. There is no tenderness. There is no rebound and no guarding.

Musculoskeletal: Normal range of motion. Lower extremity edema with minimal tenderness, nodeformity. Neurological: Pt. is alert and oriented to person, place, and time. He has no focal neurologic deficits.

Skin: Skin is warm and dry. No rash noted. Pt. is not diaphoretic. No erythema.

SF oo apristT HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Ruhl, Timothy or 4000 KRESGE WAY MRN: [PHONE-2732], DOB: 8/5/1966, Legal Sex: M Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 4/27/2021, D/C: 4/28/2021

[DOB-2733] - ED in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT (continued)

More Information

Edema

it

Edema is when you have too much fluid in your body or under your skin. Edema may make your legs, feet, and ankles swell. Swelling often happens in looser tissues, such as around your eyes. This is a common condition. It gets more common as you get older.

There are many possible causes of edema. These include:

e Eating too much salt (sodium). e Being on your feet or sitting for a long time. e Certain medical conditions, such as: o Pregnancy. Heart failure. Liver disease. Kidney disease. Cancer.

Oo O00 0

Hot weather may make edema worse. Edema is usually painless. Your skin may look swollen or shiny.

Follow these instructions at home: Medicines

e Take over-the-counter and prescription medicines only as told by your doctor. e Your doctor may prescribe a medicine to help your body get rid of extra water (diuretic). Take this medicine if you are told to take it.

Eating and drinking e Eat a low-salt (low-sodium) diet as told by your doctor. Sometimes, eating less salt may reduce swelling. e Depending on the cause of your swelling, you may need to limit how much fluid you drink (fluid restriction).

General instructions

e Raise the injured area above the level of your heart while you are sitting or lying down. Printed on 9/16/25 9:06 AM Page 609




SF oo apristT HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Ruhl, Timothy or 4000 KRESGE WAY MRN: [PHONE-2734], DOB: 8/5/1966, Legal Sex: M Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 4/27/2021, D/C: 4/28/2021

[DOB-2735] - ED in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT (continued)

More Information (continued)

Do not sit still or stand for a long time.

Do not wear tight clothes. Do not wear garters on your upper legs.

Exercise your legs. This can help the swelling go down.

Wear compression stockings as told by your doctor. It is important that these are the right size. These should be prescribed by your doctor to prevent possible injuries.

e lf elastic bandages or wraps are recommended, use them as told by your doctor.

Contact a doctor if:

e Treatment is not working. e You have heart, liver, or kidney disease and have symptoms of edema. e You have sudden and unexplained weight gain.

Get help right away if:

You have shortness of breath or chest pain.

You cannot breathe when you lie down.

You have pain, redness, or warmth in the swollen areas.

You have heart, liver, or kidney disease and get edema all of a sudden. You have a fever and your symptoms get worse all of a sudden.

These symptoms may be an emergency. Get help right away. Call 911.

e Do not wait to see if the symptoms will go away. e Do not drive yourself to the hospital.

Summary

e Edema is when you have too much fluid in your body or under your skin.

e Edema may make your legs, feet, and ankles swell. Swelling often happens in looser tissues, such as around your eyes.

e Raise the injured area above the level of your heart while you are sitting or lying down.

e Follow your doctor's instructions about diet and how much fluid you can drink.

This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss any questions you have with your health care provider.

Document Revised: [DOB-2736] Document Reviewed: [DOB-2737] Elsevier Patient Education © 2024 Elsevier Inc.

Call 911 or have someone take you to the Emergency Department if you have any of the following: Sudden numbness or weakness of your face, arm or leg especially on one side of the body Sudden confusion, difficulty speaking or trouble understanding

Changes in your vision or loss of sight in one eye

Sudden severe headache with no known cause

Sudden dizziness, trouble walking, loss of balance or coordination

It is important to seek emergency care right away if you have further stroke symptoms. If you get

[DOB-2739] - ED in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT (continued)

emergency help quickly, the powerful clot-dissolving medicines can reduce the disabilities caused by a stroke.

For more information: American Stroke Association 1-888-4-STROKE www.strokeassociation.org

IF YOU SMOKE, VAPE OR USE TOBACCO PLEASE READ THE FOLLOWING:

Why is smoking bad for me?

Smoking increases the risk of heart disease, lung disease, vascular disease, stroke, and cancer. If you smoke, STOP!

For more information:

Quit Now Kentucky 1-800-QUIT-NOW https://kentucky.quitlogix.org/en-US/

If you feel like life is too tough and are thinking of suicide or injuring yourself, get help right away! e Call or text 988 to speak to someone.

HISTORY: 54-year-old male with dyspnea on exertion.

were obtained. Compared with chest CT from [DOB-2793].

pulmonary thromboemboli. The distal pulmonary artery branches are suboptimally opacified. There are no pulmonary airspace opacities and there are no pleural or pericardial effusions.
