Performed: 08/05/20 0900 - 08/05/20 0911 Resulting lab: BAPTIST HEALTH RADIOLOGY Narrative:

CT ANGIOGRAM OF THE CHEST. MULTIPLE CORONAL, SAGITTAL, AND 3-D RECONSTRUCTIONS.

HISTORY: 54-year-old male with dyspnea on exertion.

TECHNIQUE: Radiation dose reduction techniques were utilized, including automated exposure control and exposure modulation based on body size. CT angiogram of the chest was performed following the administration of IV contrast. Multiple coronal, sagittal, and 3-D reconstruction images

were obtained. Compared with chest CT from [DOB-2796].

FINDINGS: There is adequate opacification of the main pulmonary arteries and proximal segmental branches, and there is no convincing evidence for pulmonary thromboemboli. The distal pulmonary artery branches are suboptimally opacified. There are no pulmonary airspace opacities and there are no pleural or pericardial effusions. There are mild thoracic

aortic atherosclerotic changes with plaque predominantly at the aortic

arch There is no lymphadenopathy within the chest. There is no hiatal hernia. There is significant fatty infiltration of the liver.

Impression:

There is no convincing evidence for pulmonary thromboemboli. No acute abnormality is seen.

/./ (2 VISIT #: 91085381802 08/05/20 BAPTIST _ ee DOB: 08/05/66 S54y M MRN: [PHONE-2814] RADIOLOGY ConseNT FoR nTRavenous conTasriNdecriON ll) MMIII 91085381802

My physician has scheduled me for an X-ray examination that requires an injection of contrast. The contrast (also called “X-ray dye”) appears white on the X-ray and CT scan films and helps the radiologist view my internal organs more clearly.

A small needle/catheter is inserted in a vein, usually on the inside of the arm near the elbow or on the back of the hand in most cases. The contrast is then injected by syringe or by a mechanical contrast injection unit.

Contrast is considered to be quite safe. However, adverse reactions are possible with any injection. Contrast may ooze into the soft tissue at the needle site and cause temporary discomfort and swelling. Occasionally, the injection may produce temporary irritation of the vein known as phlebitis. Occasionally, a patient may experience a mild allergic reaction to the contrast, such as sneezing, hives, and itching. Rarely, a patient may develop a serious reaction, such as impaired breathing or heartbeat irregularities. Medical treatment is available in the Radiology Department if needed. Very rarely, the contrast reaction can be so severe that death can result. | understand that there is no means of testing a patient for an allergic reaction before injecting the contrast.

A contrast agent known as “low osmolar” or “non-ionic” contrast media will be used for diagnostic x-ray and CAT scan studies. If you are having a MRI exam, a contrast agent known as “gadolinium” will be used. The contrast agents usually cause few serious reactions and are considered very safe and effective. However, | understand that medicine is not an exact science and no guarantees are being made to me concerning the results of this procedure. If complications occur, | understand that emergency medical treatment is available

| have read, or had read to me, this consent form and | understand what | am signing. | have had the opportunity to ask questions, all of which have been answered to my satisfaction. | hereby consent to the use of contrast tin | further consent to any/procedure or treatment, which may be necessary, if complications aya

Call 911 or have someone take you to the Emergency Department if you have any of the following: Sudden numbness or weakness of your face, arm or leg especially on one side of the body Sudden confusion, difficulty speaking or trouble understanding

Changes in your vision or loss of sight in one eye

Sudden severe headache with no known cause

Sudden dizziness, trouble walking, loss of balance or coordination

Call 911 or have someone take you to the Emergency Department if you have any of the following: Sudden numbness or weakness of your face, arm or leg especially on one side of the body Sudden confusion, difficulty speaking or trouble understanding

Changes in your vision or loss of sight in one eye

Sudden severe headache with no known cause

Sudden dizziness, trouble walking, loss of balance or coordination

Resulting lab: BAPTIST HEALTH RADIOLOGY

Narrative:

CT CHEST WITHOUT CONTRAST, HIGH RESOLUTION CHEST CT

HISTORY: 53-year-old male with a cough.

TECHNIQUE: Radiation dose reduction techniques were utilized, including automated exposure control and exposure modulation based on body size. 3 mm images were obtained through the chest without the administration of

IV contrast. 1 mm high-resolution images were obtained in the prone

position. There is no prior for comparison.

FINDINGS: There is significant breathing artifact on the supine series. On the prone series, there is no significant emphysematous change and there is no evidence for bronchiectasis, bronchiolectasis, subpleural

Printed on 9/16/25 9:06 AM

Page 666




[DOB-2991] - CT lou chest hi resolution in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE CT (continued)

Imaging (continued)

reticulation, or honeycombing. There is a mosaic pattern of density on some of the supine series which were obtained during expiration. There are no pleural or pericardial effusions. There is no lymphadenopathy within the chest. There is significant fatty infiltration of the liver.

There is no hiatal hernia.

Impression:

1. There is no evidence for interstitial lung disease, but there is likely scattered air trapping within both lung fields.

2. Significant hepatic steatosis.

This report was finalized on 7/20/2020 3:55 PM by Dr. Anna Kogan, M.D.

Performed: 07/17/20 1610 - 07/17/20 1624 Resulting lab: BAPTIST HEALTH RADIOLOGY Narrative:

CT CHEST WITHOUT CONTRAST, HIGH RESOLUTION CHEST CT

HISTORY: 53-year-old male with a cough.

TECHNIQUE: Radiation dose reduction techniques were utilized, including automated exposure control and exposure modulation based on body size. 3 mm images were obtained through the chest without the administration of

IV contrast. 1 mm high-resolution images were obtained in the prone position. There is no prior for comparison.

FINDINGS: There is significant breathing artifact on the supine series. On the prone series, there is no significant emphysematous change and there is no evidence for bronchiectasis, bronchiolectasis, subpleural reticulation, or honeycombing. There is a mosaic pattern of density on some of the supine series which were obtained during expiration. There are no pleural or pericardial effusions. There is no lymphadenopathy within the chest. There is significant fatty infiltration of the liver.

There is no hiatal hernia.

Impression:

1. There is no evidence for interstitial lung disease, but there is likely scattered air trapping within both lung fields.

2. Significant hepatic steatosis.

“Confidential Protected Health Information May Be Enclosed”

Protected Health Care Information is personal and sensitive information related to a person’s health care, You, the recipient, are obligated to maintain it in a safe, secure and confidential manner. Unauthorized re-disclosure or failure to maintain confidentiality could subject you to penalties described in federal and state law. IMPORTANT WARNING:

Call 911 or have someone take you to the Emergency Department if you have any of the following: Sudden numbness or weakness of your face, arm or leg especially on one side of the body Sudden confusion, difficulty speaking or trouble understanding

Changes in your vision or loss of sight in one eye

Sudden severe headache with no known cause

Sudden dizziness, trouble walking, loss of balance or coordination

[DOB-3075] - Transcribe Orders in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE PATIENT ACCESS (continued) Instructions for After Discharge

Patient Instructions None

Call 911 or have someone take you to the Emergency Department if you have any of the following: Sudden numbness or weakness of your face, arm or leg especially on one side of the body Sudden confusion, difficulty speaking or trouble understanding

Changes in your vision or loss of sight in one eye

Sudden severe headache with no known cause

Sudden dizziness, trouble walking, loss of balance or coordination

It is important to seek emergency care right away if you have further stroke symptoms. If you get emergency help quickly, the powerful clot-dissolving medicines can reduce the disabilities caused by a stroke.

For more information: American Stroke Association 1-888-4-STROKE www.strokeassociation.org
